3: Global food and energy demands are increasing mostly due to pop- ulation growth (Popp et al., 2017; Riahi et al., 2017). At the same time, the world urges for national agendas aiming at mitigation of GHG emis- sions to deaccelerate climate change (Roelfsema et al., 2020; Van Soest et al., 2021), that is already negatively impacting food security and put- ting pressure on land resources (Babiker et al., 2022; Shukla et al.,
2019). Possible solutions to ensure food security while mitigating GHG emissions include improved cropland management and efficient live- stock systems (IPCC, 2022; Doelman et al., 2019).

Brazil is among the land-rich countries with predicted expansion of agricultural commodities (food and energy crops) to help to meet grow- ing global demands associated with population growth (MAPA, 2020). The country is an important player in global beef meat (IBGE, 2021a) and bioenergy production (RFA, 2021), and it is likely to remain as one of the largest biofuel producers considering the expected produc- tion in the next decades (MAPA, 2020; MME, 2021). In this context, bioenergy-livestock integration has gained attention as a promising land-based option to mitigate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions while also alleviating pressure on land use (Souza et al., 2021a). This integra- tion takes advantage of possible synergies using biofuel by-products as cattle feed to intensify livestock production, as it can release pasture area to crops production. Usually, cattle on pasture need feed supple- mentation during dry season and the finishing cycle can happen in

Correspondence to: T.L. Junqueira, Brazilian Biorenewables National Laboratory (LNBR), Brazilian Center for Research in Energy and Materials (CNPEM), 13083-970 Campinas, Sao Paulo, Brazil. E-mail addresses: n164876@dac.unicamp.br. (N.R.D. Souza), tassia.junqueira@Inbr.cnpem.br (T.L. Junqueira).
feedlots with feed containing bioenergy by-products, grains and fibers (Souza et al., 2019).

Conventional biofuel pathways can generate by-products with high nutritional value, such as distillers' grain with solubles (DGS) from wheat and corn ethanol (Moreira et al., 2020; Milanez et al., 2014), pulp and stillage from sugar beet ethanol (Buchspies and Kaltschmitt, 2016), sugarcane bagasse, molasses and yeast in the case of sugarcane ethanol (Egeskog et al., 2011; Souza et al., 2019), and protein-rich by- products from biodiesel production from oil crops such as soybean (Souza and Seabra, 2013, 2014), palm oil and rapeseed (Corré et al., 2016). These studies accounted for opportunities of using biofuels by- products as animal feed and associated potentials to reduce land use to produce grains and cereals for animal feed. In Brazil potential options of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems (BLI) include by-products from the three main crops for biofuels production in the country: sugar- cane ethanol (CONAB, 2020), corn ethanol (CONAB, 2020) and soybean biodiesel (ANP, 2021).

There is still a clear need for customized quantitative assessments of techno-economic and environmental impacts of BLI systems, going beyond climate change impacts (Souza et al., 2021a; Moreira et al., 2020). We address this issue by comparing conventional systems with different strategies of bioenergy-livestock integration in Brazil, applying life cycle assessment (LCA) and techno-economic assessment (TEA) methodologies. We benefit from a life cycle thinking to interpret the environmental impacts towards the achievement of the Sustainable De- velopment Goals (Souza et al., 2022; Cavalett and Cherubini, 2018). This study explores the key synergies, tradeoffs, and possible adverse effects of BLI systems when compared to conventional systems and unravels their potentials and barriers for large scale implementation in the coun- try and/or other regions of the world.

7: <header>2. Methodology </header>
2.1. Modelling and simulation of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems

The BLI scenarios are designed considering possible options for live- stock intensification and use of biofuels by-products (e.g., sugarcane ba- gasse, molasses, yeast, DGS, soybean meal) as animal feed supplement, in a land management perspective based on positive experiences in Brazil and in other countries, such as the United States. Unlike DGS and soybean meal traditionally used as animal feed, sugarcane by- products have different destinations. Sugarcane bagasse is currently used to generate steam and electricity in sugarcane biorefineries, molas- ses is used in ethanol production (Souza et al., 2021a), and yeasts, that are not recycled, normally goes to vinasse (Moraes et al., 2015), how- ever, they have nutritional value as animal feed (Souza et al., 2021a). The integration is carried out by expanding biofuel crops on released pasture area. This release of pasture area is only possible due to the use of biofuel by-products as cattle feed, that are fed on feedlots.

Simulations of BLI supply-chain are performed on the Virtual Biorefinery (VB) framework (Bonomi et al., 2016), developed by the Brazilian Biorenewables National Laboratory (LNBR) and adapted to the scope of this work (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). This frame- work allows simulating the whole supply-chain, from biomass produc- tion, logistics, and industrial operations to convert biomass into products. The agricultural stage is modeled in the VB using CanaSoft tool, an excel based series of spreadsheets considering all inputs and fuels used in all the agricultural operation, such as soil preparation, planting, harvesting, application of fertilizers, agrochemicals, and use of industrial residues in the field. Although developed initially for sugarcane, CanaSoft® has been customized to represent other crops, including corn and soybean. The industrial operations are modeled con- sidering mass and energy balances from VB sugarcane biorefineries, while additional operations for corn and soybean processing were based on literature (Table S1, Supplementary Material). The logistics in- cluded the transportation distances and consumption of fuels to deliver

inputs for agricultural stage of production, biomass for biorefinery, and residues back to the field. For this study, the finishing stage of beef cattle value chain and feed production on biorefineries are inserted and modeled on the VB using data based both in literature and exemplary BLI cases in real world. The life cycle inventories and inputs for cash flow analysis from agricultural and industrial modelling are used to as- sess techno-economic feasibility and environmental assessment using LCA methodology.

We considered the three main biofuel crops in the country. For in- stance, 90% of total 30 billion 1 of ethanol produced in Brazil is from sug- arcane, the remaining volume is produced from corn (CONAB, 2020). Total sugarcane production account for around 10 million ha (IBGE, 2021b), about half of it used for ethanol (CONAB, 2020). Total area of corn in Brazil is around 18 million ha (IBGE, 2021c). Around 74% of the 5 billion 1 of biodiesel produced in the country is from soybean oil (ANP, 2021) and total soybean area in the country is about 36 million ha (IBGE, 2021c).

We assessed seven scenarios, considering different levels of integra- tion with sugarcane, corn and soybean (Table 1). The first scenario is a base of comparison with beef cattle and sugarcane ethanol produced in conventional systems, without any integration (Base). Conventional scenarios include cattle finished in grass-fed pasture systems. BLI sce- narios consider cattle finished in feedlots with biorefineries producing feed ingredients. Feedlot area is considered neglectable because it typi- cally represents less than 1% of total study area. BLI scenarios consider crop expansion (sugarcane area, plus corn in rotation with soybean area) on released pasture area. Corn and soybean rotation happens by using the same area in the same year to plant and harvest both crops se- quentially in different periods of the year, a very common practice in Brazil (Magalhães et al., 2020). Main parameters used to model the assessed scenarios are presented in Table 2. Additional information about industrial conversion systems is presented in Table S2 (Supple- mentary Material).

Scenario Sug_feed considers a sugarcane plant integrated to beef cattle production and sugarcane by-products (i.e., bagasse and yeast) used as part of animal feed. Using bagasse as animal feed reduces elec- tricity production but using bleed yeasts has no impacts in the biorefinery. The necessary corn area in is calculated based on feed requirements. Soybean is produced in rotation with corn, and part of soybean meal produced in this area composes animal feed, while soybean oil and surplus meal are sold to the market. The equiva- lent crop area is considered in Sug_past, but the sugarcane plant does not produce any feed, therefore all soybean meal, soybean oil and corn grain are marketed.

In scenarios Corn_past and Corn_feed, corn ethanol plants are annexed to sugarcane plants and operate during sugarcane offseason (130 days), in the so-called "flex" ethanol plants. Corn ethanol plants use energy provided by extended operation of sugarcane combined heat and power generation (CHP) unit that runs with sugarcane ligno- cellulosic material (LCM) (i.e., bagasse and straw) during sugarcane off-season. Daily sugarcane ethanol production volume determines the corn ethanol plant size since they use the same distillation and de- hydration facilities. Corn processing capacity defines corn area. Corn ethanol is produced using dry-grind process. Ethanol is the main prod- uct, and the DGS co-products are dried, becoming DDGS, and are used as animal feed (USGC, 2012). In these two scenarios corn grain is replaced in the feed formulation by DDGS in a proportion of 1:1 (Hoffman and Baker, 2011). As in Sug_feed, surplus soybean meal and soybean oil are sold. Scenario Corn_past has the equivalent sugarcane, corn, and soybean areas, but without any integration.

The difference between Corn scenarios (Corn_feed and Corn_past) and Soy scenarios (Soy_feed Soy_past) is that the latter uses all soy- bean oil to produce biodiesel integrated with sugarcane ethanol, which is partially used in the transesterification (Olivério et al., 2014; Souza and Seabra, 2013, Scenario have an equiv- alent crop area, but only Soy_feed in feedlots.

1 Junqueira et al. (2016). 2 Moraes et al. (2014). 3 Bonomi et al. (2016). 4 Sampaio et al. (2019). 5 Souza et al. (2019). 6 Bonomi et al. (2019). 7 Olivério et al. (2014). 8 Dias et al. (2016). 9 Milanez et al. (2014). 10 Matsuura and Picoli (2018).

In all scenarios, sugarcane processing capacity is 4 million tonnes/ year, producing anhydrous ethanol and electricity. Electricity is pro- duced from sugarcane bagasse, straw and biogas produced from anaer- obic digestion of sugarcane vinasse (Moraes et al., 2014; Junqueira et al., 2016). Around 120 kg of straw is produced per tonne of sugarcane (Menandro et al., 2017) and 50% of it is recovered from the fields using bales system (Carvalho et al., 2017). At the mill, sugarcane straw is washed and crushed, reaching a 50% moisture (equally to bagasse), subsequently it is sent to the CHP unit where it is mixed with bagasse (Mantelatto et al., 2020). All scenarios include a plant to extract soybean oil, obtaining soymeal as coproduct. Both sugarcane and soybean pro- cessing plants operate 200 days per year.

Feed composition is based on Souza et al. (2019) and presented in Table S4 (Supplementary Material). To produce feed in the BLI scenar- ios, part of bagasse goes through a pretreatment process with steam ex- plosion to increase its nutritional value as beef cattle feed (Souza et al., 2019). The yeast from fermentation bleed is diverted to animal feed pro- duction. After meeting internal heat and power demands, sugarcane LCM can be diverted to feed, corn ethanol and biodiesel production. Sugarcane molasses, a by-product from sugar production, represents about 2% of feed composition and it is purchased from other sugarcane processing facilities with an annexed sugar production process.

Our assumptions for modelling the livestock system are simplified in face of the many specificities of the sector. For example, we consider a stabilized herd, with all animals at the same age and weight at the be- ginning and end of the finishing cycle. For conventional systems we consider an extensive management with a stocking rate of one head per hectare (EMBRAPA, 2014). In BLI scenarios, we assume that beef meat production would not be negatively affected by the crop's expan- sion, nor additional pasture area would be necessary. The number of

cattle heads is defined considering that for each hectare of released land for crop production, one cattle head should be fed with animal feed produced inside the system boundaries. The processing capacity of sugarcane plants define the sugarcane agricultural area and the corn processing capacity, consequently, corn/soybean agricultural area. In this way, cattle heads are equal to total hectares of crops production in BLI systems.

The key BLI aspects include: a) manure replacing part of mineral ni- trogen fertilizer (i.e. urea); b) sugarcane plant providing power and heat for soybean and corn processing plants; c) soybean extraction and anaerobic digestion plant operate during the 200 days of sugarcane season; d) corn ethanol plant operates during 130 days in sugarcane off- season; e) biogas from anaerobic digestion of vinasse is burnt to gener- ate electricity; f) yeast from sugarcane ethanol fermentation is used in the animal feed formulation; and g) ethanol is used for biodiesel transesterification. Although methanol is frequently used (EPE, 2019), we consider use of ethanol to maximize the integration, as in Olivério et al. (2014).
8: <header>2. Methodology </header>
2.1. Modelling and simulation of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems

The BLI scenarios are designed considering possible options for live- stock intensification and use of biofuels by-products (e.g., sugarcane ba- gasse, molasses, yeast, DGS, soybean meal) as animal feed supplement, in a land management perspective based on positive experiences in Brazil and in other countries, such as the United States. Unlike DGS and soybean meal traditionally used as animal feed, sugarcane by- products have different destinations. Sugarcane bagasse is currently used to generate steam and electricity in sugarcane biorefineries, molas- ses is used in ethanol production (Souza et al., 2021a), and yeasts, that are not recycled, normally goes to vinasse (Moraes et al., 2015), how- ever, they have nutritional value as animal feed (Souza et al., 2021a). The integration is carried out by expanding biofuel crops on released pasture area. This release of pasture area is only possible due to the use of biofuel by-products as cattle feed, that are fed on feedlots.

Simulations of BLI supply-chain are performed on the Virtual Biorefinery (VB) framework (Bonomi et al., 2016), developed by the Brazilian Biorenewables National Laboratory (LNBR) and adapted to the scope of this work (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). This frame- work allows simulating the whole supply-chain, from biomass produc- tion, logistics, and industrial operations to convert biomass into products. The agricultural stage is modeled in the VB using CanaSoft tool, an excel based series of spreadsheets considering all inputs and fuels used in all the agricultural operation, such as soil preparation, planting, harvesting, application of fertilizers, agrochemicals, and use of industrial residues in the field. Although developed initially for sugarcane, CanaSoft® has been customized to represent other crops, including corn and soybean. The industrial operations are modeled con- sidering mass and energy balances from VB sugarcane biorefineries, while additional operations for corn and soybean processing were based on literature (Table S1, Supplementary Material). The logistics in- cluded the transportation distances and consumption of fuels to deliver

inputs for agricultural stage of production, biomass for biorefinery, and residues back to the field. For this study, the finishing stage of beef cattle value chain and feed production on biorefineries are inserted and modeled on the VB using data based both in literature and exemplary BLI cases in real world. The life cycle inventories and inputs for cash flow analysis from agricultural and industrial modelling are used to as- sess techno-economic feasibility and environmental assessment using LCA methodology.

We considered the three main biofuel crops in the country. For in- stance, 90% of total 30 billion 1 of ethanol produced in Brazil is from sug- arcane, the remaining volume is produced from corn (CONAB, 2020). Total sugarcane production account for around 10 million ha (IBGE, 2021b), about half of it used for ethanol (CONAB, 2020). Total area of corn in Brazil is around 18 million ha (IBGE, 2021c). Around 74% of the 5 billion 1 of biodiesel produced in the country is from soybean oil (ANP, 2021) and total soybean area in the country is about 36 million ha (IBGE, 2021c).

We assessed seven scenarios, considering different levels of integra- tion with sugarcane, corn and soybean (Table 1). The first scenario is a base of comparison with beef cattle and sugarcane ethanol produced in conventional systems, without any integration (Base). Conventional scenarios include cattle finished in grass-fed pasture systems. BLI sce- narios consider cattle finished in feedlots with biorefineries producing feed ingredients. Feedlot area is considered neglectable because it typi- cally represents less than 1% of total study area. BLI scenarios consider crop expansion (sugarcane area, plus corn in rotation with soybean area) on released pasture area. Corn and soybean rotation happens by using the same area in the same year to plant and harvest both crops se- quentially in different periods of the year, a very common practice in Brazil (Magalhães et al., 2020). Main parameters used to model the assessed scenarios are presented in Table 2. Additional information about industrial conversion systems is presented in Table S2 (Supple- mentary Material).

Scenario Sug_feed considers a sugarcane plant integrated to beef cattle production and sugarcane by-products (i.e., bagasse and yeast) used as part of animal feed. Using bagasse as animal feed reduces elec- tricity production but using bleed yeasts has no impacts in the biorefinery. The necessary corn area in is calculated based on feed requirements. Soybean is produced in rotation with corn, and part of soybean meal produced in this area composes animal feed, while soybean oil and surplus meal are sold to the market. The equiva- lent crop area is considered in Sug_past, but the sugarcane plant does not produce any feed, therefore all soybean meal, soybean oil and corn grain are marketed.

In scenarios Corn_past and Corn_feed, corn ethanol plants are annexed to sugarcane plants and operate during sugarcane offseason (130 days), in the so-called "flex" ethanol plants. Corn ethanol plants use energy provided by extended operation of sugarcane combined heat and power generation (CHP) unit that runs with sugarcane ligno- cellulosic material (LCM) (i.e., bagasse and straw) during sugarcane off-season. Daily sugarcane ethanol production volume determines the corn ethanol plant size since they use the same distillation and de- hydration facilities. Corn processing capacity defines corn area. Corn ethanol is produced using dry-grind process. Ethanol is the main prod- uct, and the DGS co-products are dried, becoming DDGS, and are used as animal feed (USGC, 2012). In these two scenarios corn grain is replaced in the feed formulation by DDGS in a proportion of 1:1 (Hoffman and Baker, 2011). As in Sug_feed, surplus soybean meal and soybean oil are sold. Scenario Corn_past has the equivalent sugarcane, corn, and soybean areas, but without any integration.

The difference between Corn scenarios (Corn_feed and Corn_past) and Soy scenarios (Soy_feed Soy_past) is that the latter uses all soy- bean oil to produce biodiesel integrated with sugarcane ethanol, which is partially used in the transesterification (Olivério et al., 2014; Souza and Seabra, 2013, Scenario have an equiv- alent crop area, but only Soy_feed in feedlots.

For conventional systems we consider an extensive management with a stocking rate of one head per hectare (EMBRAPA, 2014). In BLI scenarios, we assume that beef meat production would not be negatively affected by the crop's expan- sion, nor additional pasture area would be necessary. The number of

cattle heads is defined considering that for each hectare of released land for crop production, one cattle head should be fed with animal feed produced inside the system boundaries. The processing capacity of sugarcane plants define the sugarcane agricultural area and the corn processing capacity, consequently, corn/soybean agricultural area. In this way, cattle heads are equal to total hectares of crops production in BLI systems.

The key BLI aspects include: a) manure replacing part of mineral ni- trogen fertilizer (i.e. urea); b) sugarcane plant providing power and heat for soybean and corn processing plants; c) soybean extraction and anaerobic digestion plant operate during the 200 days of sugarcane season; d) corn ethanol plant operates during 130 days in sugarcane off- season; e) biogas from anaerobic digestion of vinasse is burnt to gener- ate electricity; f) yeast from sugarcane ethanol fermentation is used in the animal feed formulation; and g) ethanol is used for biodiesel transesterification. Although methanol is frequently used (EPE, 2019), we consider use of ethanol to maximize the integration, as in Olivério et al. (2014).
10: <header>2. Methodology </header>
2.1. Modelling and simulation of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems

The BLI scenarios are designed considering possible options for live- stock intensification and use of biofuels by-products (e.g., sugarcane ba- gasse, molasses, yeast, DGS, soybean meal) as animal feed supplement, in a land management perspective based on positive experiences in Brazil and in other countries, such as the United States. Unlike DGS and soybean meal traditionally used as animal feed, sugarcane by- products have different destinations. Sugarcane bagasse is currently used to generate steam and electricity in sugarcane biorefineries, molas- ses is used in ethanol production (Souza et al., 2021a), and yeasts, that are not recycled, normally goes to vinasse (Moraes et al., 2015), how- ever, they have nutritional value as animal feed (Souza et al., 2021a). The integration is carried out by expanding biofuel crops on released pasture area. This release of pasture area is only possible due to the use of biofuel by-products as cattle feed, that are fed on feedlots.

Simulations of BLI supply-chain are performed on the Virtual Biorefinery (VB) framework (Bonomi et al., 2016), developed by the Brazilian Biorenewables National Laboratory (LNBR) and adapted to the scope of this work (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). This frame- work allows simulating the whole supply-chain, from biomass produc- tion, logistics, and industrial operations to convert biomass into products. The agricultural stage is modeled in the VB using CanaSoft tool, an excel based series of spreadsheets considering all inputs and fuels used in all the agricultural operation, such as soil preparation, planting, harvesting, application of fertilizers, agrochemicals, and use of industrial residues in the field. Although developed initially for sugarcane, CanaSoft® has been customized to represent other crops, including corn and soybean. The industrial operations are modeled con- sidering mass and energy balances from VB sugarcane biorefineries, while additional operations for corn and soybean processing were based on literature (Table S1, Supplementary Material). The logistics in- cluded the transportation distances and consumption of fuels to deliver

inputs for agricultural stage of production, biomass for biorefinery, and residues back to the field. For this study, the finishing stage of beef cattle value chain and feed production on biorefineries are inserted and modeled on the VB using data based both in literature and exemplary BLI cases in real world. The life cycle inventories and inputs for cash flow analysis from agricultural and industrial modelling are used to as- sess techno-economic feasibility and environmental assessment using LCA methodology.

We considered the three main biofuel crops in the country. For in- stance, 90% of total 30 billion 1 of ethanol produced in Brazil is from sug- arcane, the remaining volume is produced from corn (CONAB, 2020). Total sugarcane production account for around 10 million ha (IBGE, 2021b), about half of it used for ethanol (CONAB, 2020). Total area of corn in Brazil is around 18 million ha (IBGE, 2021c). Around 74% of the 5 billion 1 of biodiesel produced in the country is from soybean oil (ANP, 2021) and total soybean area in the country is about 36 million ha (IBGE, 2021c).

We assessed seven scenarios, considering different levels of integra- tion with sugarcane, corn and soybean (Table 1). The first scenario is a base of comparison with beef cattle and sugarcane ethanol produced in conventional systems, without any integration (Base). Conventional scenarios include cattle finished in grass-fed pasture systems. BLI sce- narios consider cattle finished in feedlots with biorefineries producing feed ingredients. Feedlot area is considered neglectable because it typi- cally represents less than 1% of total study area. BLI scenarios consider crop expansion (sugarcane area, plus corn in rotation with soybean area) on released pasture area. Corn and soybean rotation happens by using the same area in the same year to plant and harvest both crops se- quentially in different periods of the year, a very common practice in Brazil (Magalhães et al., 2020). Main parameters used to model the assessed scenarios are presented in Table 2. Additional information about industrial conversion systems is presented in Table S2 (Supple- mentary Material).

Scenario Sug_feed considers a sugarcane plant integrated to beef cattle production and sugarcane by-products (i.e., bagasse and yeast) used as part of animal feed. Using bagasse as animal feed reduces elec- tricity production but using bleed yeasts has no impacts in the biorefinery. The necessary corn area in is calculated based on feed requirements. Soybean is produced in rotation with corn, and part of soybean meal produced in this area composes animal feed, while soybean oil and surplus meal are sold to the market. The equiva- lent crop area is considered in Sug_past, but the sugarcane plant does not produce any feed, therefore all soybean meal, soybean oil and corn grain are marketed.

In scenarios Corn_past and Corn_feed, corn ethanol plants are annexed to sugarcane plants and operate during sugarcane offseason (130 days), in the so-called "flex" ethanol plants. Corn ethanol plants use energy provided by extended operation of sugarcane combined heat and power generation (CHP) unit that runs with sugarcane ligno- cellulosic material (LCM) (i.e., bagasse and straw) during sugarcane off-season. Daily sugarcane ethanol production volume determines the corn ethanol plant size since they use the same distillation and de- hydration facilities. Corn processing capacity defines corn area. Corn ethanol is produced using dry-grind process. Ethanol is the main prod- uct, and the DGS co-products are dried, becoming DDGS, and are used as animal feed (USGC, 2012). In these two scenarios corn grain is replaced in the feed formulation by DDGS in a proportion of 1:1 (Hoffman and Baker, 2011). As in Sug_feed, surplus soybean meal and soybean oil are sold. Scenario Corn_past has the equivalent sugarcane, corn, and soybean areas, but without any integration.

The difference between Corn scenarios (Corn_feed and Corn_past) and Soy scenarios (Soy_feed Soy_past) is that the latter uses all soy- bean oil to produce biodiesel integrated with sugarcane ethanol, which is partially used in the transesterification (Olivério et al., 2014; Souza and Seabra, 2013, Scenario have an equiv- alent crop area, but only Soy_feed in feedlots.

In all scenarios, sugarcane processing capacity is 4 million tonnes/ year, producing anhydrous ethanol and electricity. Electricity is pro- duced from sugarcane bagasse, straw and biogas produced from anaer- obic digestion of sugarcane vinasse (Moraes et al., 2014; Junqueira et al., 2016). Around 120 kg of straw is produced per tonne of sugarcane (Menandro et al., 2017) and 50% of it is recovered from the fields using bales system (Carvalho et al., 2017). At the mill, sugarcane straw is washed and crushed, reaching a 50% moisture (equally to bagasse), subsequently it is sent to the CHP unit where it is mixed with bagasse (Mantelatto et al., 2020). All scenarios include a plant to extract soybean oil, obtaining soymeal as coproduct. Both sugarcane and soybean pro- cessing plants operate 200 days per year.

Feed composition is based on Souza et al. (2019) and presented in Table S4 (Supplementary Material). To produce feed in the BLI scenar- ios, part of bagasse goes through a pretreatment process with steam ex- plosion to increase its nutritional value as beef cattle feed (Souza et al., 2019). The yeast from fermentation bleed is diverted to animal feed pro- duction. After meeting internal heat and power demands, sugarcane LCM can be diverted to feed, corn ethanol and biodiesel production. Sugarcane molasses, a by-product from sugar production, represents about 2% of feed composition and it is purchased from other sugarcane processing facilities with an annexed sugar production process.

For conventional systems we consider an extensive management with a stocking rate of one head per hectare (EMBRAPA, 2014). The agricultural stage is modeled in the VB using CanaSoft tool, an excel based series of spreadsheets considering all inputs and fuels used in all the agricultural operation, such as soil preparation, planting, harvesting, application of fertilizers, agrochemicals, and use of industrial residues in the field. Although developed initially for sugarcane, CanaSoft® has been customized to represent other crops, including corn and soybean. The industrial operations are modeled con- sidering mass and energy balances from VB sugarcane biorefineries, while additional operations for corn and soybean processing were based on literature (Table S1, Supplementary Material). The logistics in- cluded the transportation distances and consumption of fuels to deliver

inputs for agricultural stage of production, biomass for biorefinery, and residues back to the field. For this study, the finishing stage of beef cattle value chain and feed production on biorefineries are inserted and modeled on the VB using data based both in literature and exemplary BLI cases in real world. The life cycle inventories and inputs for cash flow analysis from agricultural and industrial modelling are used to as- sess techno-economic feasibility and environmental assessment using LCA methodology.

We considered the three main biofuel crops in the country. For in- stance, 90% of total 30 billion 1 of ethanol produced in Brazil is from sug- arcane, the remaining volume is produced from corn (CONAB, 2020). Total sugarcane production account for around 10 million ha (IBGE, 2021b), about half of it used for ethanol (CONAB, 2020). Total area of corn in Brazil is around 18 million ha (IBGE, 2021c). Around 74% of the 5 billion 1 of biodiesel produced in the country is from soybean oil (ANP, 2021) and total soybean area in the country is about 36 million ha (IBGE, 2021c).

We assessed seven scenarios, considering different levels of integra- tion with sugarcane, corn and soybean (Table 1). The first scenario is a base of comparison with beef cattle and sugarcane ethanol produced in conventional systems, without any integration (Base). Conventional scenarios include cattle finished in grass-fed pasture systems. BLI sce- narios consider cattle finished in feedlots with biorefineries producing feed ingredients. Feedlot area is considered neglectable because it typi- cally represents less than 1% of total study area. BLI scenarios consider crop expansion (sugarcane area, plus corn in rotation with soybean area) on released pasture area. Corn and soybean rotation happens by using the same area in the same year to plant and harvest both crops se- quentially in different periods of the year, a very common practice in Brazil (Magalhães et al., 2020). Main parameters used to model the assessed scenarios are presented in Table 2. Additional information about industrial conversion systems is presented in Table S2 (Supple- mentary Material).

Scenario Sug_feed considers a sugarcane plant integrated to beef cattle production and sugarcane by-products (i.e., bagasse and yeast) used as part of animal feed. Using bagasse as animal feed reduces elec- tricity production but using bleed yeasts has no impacts in the biorefinery. The necessary corn area in is calculated based on feed requirements. Soybean is produced in rotation with corn, and part of soybean meal produced in this area composes animal feed, while soybean oil and surplus meal are sold to the market. The equiva- lent crop area is considered in Sug_past, but the sugarcane plant does not produce any feed, therefore all soybean meal, soybean oil and corn grain are marketed.

In scenarios Corn_past and Corn_feed, corn ethanol plants are annexed to sugarcane plants and operate during sugarcane offseason (130 days), in the so-called "flex" ethanol plants. Corn ethanol plants use energy provided by extended operation of sugarcane combined heat and power generation (CHP) unit that runs with sugarcane ligno- cellulosic material (LCM) (i.e., bagasse and straw) during sugarcane off-season. Daily sugarcane ethanol production volume determines the corn ethanol plant size since they use the same distillation and de- hydration facilities. Corn processing capacity defines corn area. Corn ethanol is produced using dry-grind process. Ethanol is the main prod- uct, and the DGS co-products are dried, becoming DDGS, and are used as animal feed (USGC, 2012). In these two scenarios corn grain is replaced in the feed formulation by DDGS in a proportion of 1:1 (Hoffman and Baker, 2011). As in Sug_feed, surplus soybean meal and soybean oil are sold. Scenario Corn_past has the equivalent sugarcane, corn, and soybean areas, but without any integration.

The difference between Corn scenarios (Corn_feed and Corn_past) and Soy scenarios (Soy_feed Soy_past) is that the latter uses all soy- bean oil to produce biodiesel integrated with sugarcane ethanol, which is partially used in the transesterification (Olivério et al., 2014; Souza and Seabra, 2013, Scenario have an equiv- alent crop area, but only Soy_feed in feedlots.

1 Junqueira et al. (2016). 2 Moraes et al. (2014). 3 Bonomi et al. (2016). 4 Sampaio et al. (2019). 5 Souza et al. (2019). 6 Bonomi et al. (2019). 7 Olivério et al. (2014). 8 Dias et al. (2016). 9 Milanez et al. (2014). 10 Matsuura and Picoli (2018).

In all scenarios, sugarcane processing capacity is 4 million tonnes/ year, producing anhydrous ethanol and electricity. Electricity is pro- duced from sugarcane bagasse, straw and biogas produced from anaer- obic digestion of sugarcane vinasse (Moraes et al., 2014; Junqueira et al., 2016). Around 120 kg of straw is produced per tonne of sugarcane (Menandro et al., 2017) and 50% of it is recovered from the fields using bales system (Carvalho et al., 2017). At the mill, sugarcane straw is washed and crushed, reaching a 50% moisture (equally to bagasse), subsequently it is sent to the CHP unit where it is mixed with bagasse (Mantelatto et al., 2020). All scenarios include a plant to extract soybean oil, obtaining soymeal as coproduct. Both sugarcane and soybean pro- cessing plants operate 200 days per year.

Feed composition is based on Souza et al. (2019) and presented in Table S4 (Supplementary Material). To produce feed in the BLI scenar- ios, part of bagasse goes through a pretreatment process with steam ex- plosion to increase its nutritional value as beef cattle feed (Souza et al., 2019). The yeast from fermentation bleed is diverted to animal feed pro- duction. After meeting internal heat and power demands, sugarcane LCM can be diverted to feed, corn ethanol and biodiesel production. Sugarcane molasses, a by-product from sugar production, represents about 2% of feed composition and it is purchased from other sugarcane processing facilities with an annexed sugar production process.

Our assumptions for modelling the livestock system are simplified in face of the many specificities of the sector. For example, we consider a stabilized herd, with all animals at the same age and weight at the be- ginning and end of the finishing cycle. For conventional systems we consider an extensive management with a stocking rate of one head per hectare (EMBRAPA, 2014). In BLI scenarios, we assume that beef meat production would not be negatively affected by the crop's expan- sion, nor additional pasture area would be necessary. The number of

cattle heads is defined considering that for each hectare of released land for crop production, one cattle head should be fed with animal feed produced inside the system boundaries. The processing capacity of sugarcane plants define the sugarcane agricultural area and the corn processing capacity, consequently, corn/soybean agricultural area. In this way, cattle heads are equal to total hectares of crops production in BLI systems.

The key BLI aspects include: a) manure replacing part of mineral ni- trogen fertilizer (i.e. urea); b) sugarcane plant providing power and heat for soybean and corn processing plants; c) soybean extraction and anaerobic digestion plant operate during the 200 days of sugarcane season; d) corn ethanol plant operates during 130 days in sugarcane off- season; e) biogas from anaerobic digestion of vinasse is burnt to gener- ate electricity; f) yeast from sugarcane ethanol fermentation is used in the animal feed formulation; and g) ethanol is used for biodiesel transesterification. Although methanol is frequently used (EPE, 2019), we consider use of ethanol to maximize the integration, as in Olivério et al. (2014).
13: <header>2. Methodology </header>
2.1. Modelling and simulation of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems

The BLI scenarios are designed considering possible options for live- stock intensification and use of biofuels by-products (e.g., sugarcane ba- gasse, molasses, yeast, DGS, soybean meal) as animal feed supplement, in a land management perspective based on positive experiences in Brazil and in other countries, such as the United States. Unlike DGS and soybean meal traditionally used as animal feed, sugarcane by- products have different destinations. Sugarcane bagasse is currently used to generate steam and electricity in sugarcane biorefineries, molas- ses is used in ethanol production (Souza et al., 2021a), and yeasts, that are not recycled, normally goes to vinasse (Moraes et al., 2015), how- ever, they have nutritional value as animal feed (Souza et al., 2021a). The integration is carried out by expanding biofuel crops on released pasture area. This release of pasture area is only possible due to the use of biofuel by-products as cattle feed, that are fed on feedlots.

Simulations of BLI supply-chain are performed on the Virtual Biorefinery (VB) framework (Bonomi et al., 2016), developed by the Brazilian Biorenewables National Laboratory (LNBR) and adapted to the scope of this work (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). This frame- work allows simulating the whole supply-chain, from biomass produc- tion, logistics, and industrial operations to convert biomass into products. The agricultural stage is modeled in the VB using CanaSoft tool, an excel based series of spreadsheets considering all inputs and fuels used in all the agricultural operation, such as soil preparation, planting, harvesting, application of fertilizers, agrochemicals, and use of industrial residues in the field. Although developed initially for sugarcane, CanaSoft® has been customized to represent other crops, including corn and soybean. The industrial operations are modeled con- sidering mass and energy balances from VB sugarcane biorefineries, while additional operations for corn and soybean processing were based on literature (Table S1, Supplementary Material). The logistics in- cluded the transportation distances and consumption of fuels to deliver

inputs for agricultural stage of production, biomass for biorefinery, and residues back to the field. For this study, the finishing stage of beef cattle value chain and feed production on biorefineries are inserted and modeled on the VB using data based both in literature and exemplary BLI cases in real world. The life cycle inventories and inputs for cash flow analysis from agricultural and industrial modelling are used to as- sess techno-economic feasibility and environmental assessment using LCA methodology.

In all scenarios, sugarcane processing capacity is 4 million tonnes/ year, producing anhydrous ethanol and electricity. Electricity is pro- duced from sugarcane bagasse, straw and biogas produced from anaer- obic digestion of sugarcane vinasse (Moraes et al., 2014; Junqueira et al., 2016). Around 120 kg of straw is produced per tonne of sugarcane (Menandro et al., 2017) and 50% of it is recovered from the fields using bales system (Carvalho et al., 2017). At the mill, sugarcane straw is washed and crushed, reaching a 50% moisture (equally to bagasse), subsequently it is sent to the CHP unit where it is mixed with bagasse (Mantelatto et al., 2020). All scenarios include a plant to extract soybean oil, obtaining soymeal as coproduct. Both sugarcane and soybean pro- cessing plants operate 200 days per year.

Feed composition is based on Souza et al. (2019) and presented in Table S4 (Supplementary Material). To produce feed in the BLI scenar- ios, part of bagasse goes through a pretreatment process with steam ex- plosion to increase its nutritional value as beef cattle feed (Souza et al., 2019). The yeast from fermentation bleed is diverted to animal feed pro- duction. After meeting internal heat and power demands, sugarcane LCM can be diverted to feed, corn ethanol and biodiesel production. Sugarcane molasses, a by-product from sugar production, represents about 2% of feed composition and it is purchased from other sugarcane processing facilities with an annexed sugar production process.

Our assumptions for modelling the livestock system are simplified in face of the many specificities of the sector. For example, we consider a stabilized herd, with all animals at the same age and weight at the be- ginning and end of the finishing cycle. For conventional systems we consider an extensive management with a stocking rate of one head per hectare (EMBRAPA, 2014). In BLI scenarios, we assume that beef meat production would not be negatively affected by the crop's expan- sion, nor additional pasture area would be necessary. The number of

cattle heads is defined considering that for each hectare of released land for crop production, one cattle head should be fed with animal feed produced inside the system boundaries. The processing capacity of sugarcane plants define the sugarcane agricultural area and the corn processing capacity, consequently, corn/soybean agricultural area. In this way, cattle heads are equal to total hectares of crops production in BLI systems.

The key BLI aspects include: a) manure replacing part of mineral ni- trogen fertilizer (i.e. urea); b) sugarcane plant providing power and heat for soybean and corn processing plants; c) soybean extraction and anaerobic digestion plant operate during the 200 days of sugarcane season; d) corn ethanol plant operates during 130 days in sugarcane off- season; e) biogas from anaerobic digestion of vinasse is burnt to gener- ate electricity; f) yeast from sugarcane ethanol fermentation is used in the animal feed formulation; and g) ethanol is used for biodiesel transesterification. Although methanol is frequently used (EPE, 2019), we consider use of ethanol to maximize the integration, as in Olivério et al. (2014).
15: <header>2. Methodology </header>
2.1. Modelling and simulation of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems

The BLI scenarios are designed considering possible options for live- stock intensification and use of biofuels by-products (e.g., sugarcane ba- gasse, molasses, yeast, DGS, soybean meal) as animal feed supplement, in a land management perspective based on positive experiences in Brazil and in other countries, such as the United States. Unlike DGS and soybean meal traditionally used as animal feed, sugarcane by- products have different destinations. Sugarcane bagasse is currently used to generate steam and electricity in sugarcane biorefineries, molas- ses is used in ethanol production (Souza et al., 2021a), and yeasts, that are not recycled, normally goes to vinasse (Moraes et al., 2015), how- ever, they have nutritional value as animal feed (Souza et al., 2021a). The integration is carried out by expanding biofuel crops on released pasture area. This release of pasture area is only possible due to the use of biofuel by-products as cattle feed, that are fed on feedlots.

Simulations of BLI supply-chain are performed on the Virtual Biorefinery (VB) framework (Bonomi et al., 2016), developed by the Brazilian Biorenewables National Laboratory (LNBR) and adapted to the scope of this work (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). This frame- work allows simulating the whole supply-chain, from biomass produc- tion, logistics, and industrial operations to convert biomass into products. The agricultural stage is modeled in the VB using CanaSoft tool, an excel based series of spreadsheets considering all inputs and fuels used in all the agricultural operation, such as soil preparation, planting, harvesting, application of fertilizers, agrochemicals, and use of industrial residues in the field. Although developed initially for sugarcane, CanaSoft® has been customized to represent other crops, including corn and soybean. The industrial operations are modeled con- sidering mass and energy balances from VB sugarcane biorefineries, while additional operations for corn and soybean processing were based on literature (Table S1, Supplementary Material). The logistics in- cluded the transportation distances and consumption of fuels to deliver

inputs for agricultural stage of production, biomass for biorefinery, and residues back to the field. For this study, the finishing stage of beef cattle value chain and feed production on biorefineries are inserted and modeled on the VB using data based both in literature and exemplary BLI cases in real world. The life cycle inventories and inputs for cash flow analysis from agricultural and industrial modelling are used to as- sess techno-economic feasibility and environmental assessment using LCA methodology.

We considered the three main biofuel crops in the country. For in- stance, 90% of total 30 billion 1 of ethanol produced in Brazil is from sug- arcane, the remaining volume is produced from corn (CONAB, 2020). Total sugarcane production account for around 10 million ha (IBGE, 2021b), about half of it used for ethanol (CONAB, 2020). Total area of corn in Brazil is around 18 million ha (IBGE, 2021c). Around 74% of the 5 billion 1 of biodiesel produced in the country is from soybean oil (ANP, 2021) and total soybean area in the country is about 36 million ha (IBGE, 2021c).

We assessed seven scenarios, considering different levels of integra- tion with sugarcane, corn and soybean (Table 1). The first scenario is a base of comparison with beef cattle and sugarcane ethanol produced in conventional systems, without any integration (Base). Conventional scenarios include cattle finished in grass-fed pasture systems. BLI sce- narios consider cattle finished in feedlots with biorefineries producing feed ingredients. Feedlot area is considered neglectable because it typi- cally represents less than 1% of total study area. BLI scenarios consider crop expansion (sugarcane area, plus corn in rotation with soybean area) on released pasture area. Corn and soybean rotation happens by using the same area in the same year to plant and harvest both crops se- quentially in different periods of the year, a very common practice in Brazil (Magalhães et al., 2020). Main parameters used to model the assessed scenarios are presented in Table 2. Additional information about industrial conversion systems is presented in Table S2 (Supple- mentary Material).

Scenario Sug_feed considers a sugarcane plant integrated to beef cattle production and sugarcane by-products (i.e., bagasse and yeast) used as part of animal feed. Using bagasse as animal feed reduces elec- tricity production but using bleed yeasts has no impacts in the biorefinery. The necessary corn area in is calculated based on feed requirements. Soybean is produced in rotation with corn, and part of soybean meal produced in this area composes animal feed, while soybean oil and surplus meal are sold to the market. The equiva- lent crop area is considered in Sug_past, but the sugarcane plant does not produce any feed, therefore all soybean meal, soybean oil and corn grain are marketed.

In scenarios Corn_past and Corn_feed, corn ethanol plants are annexed to sugarcane plants and operate during sugarcane offseason (130 days), in the so-called "flex" ethanol plants. Corn ethanol plants use energy provided by extended operation of sugarcane combined heat and power generation (CHP) unit that runs with sugarcane ligno- cellulosic material (LCM) (i.e., bagasse and straw) during sugarcane off-season. Daily sugarcane ethanol production volume determines the corn ethanol plant size since they use the same distillation and de- hydration facilities. Corn processing capacity defines corn area. Corn ethanol is produced using dry-grind process. Ethanol is the main prod- uct, and the DGS co-products are dried, becoming DDGS, and are used as animal feed (USGC, 2012). In these two scenarios corn grain is replaced in the feed formulation by DDGS in a proportion of 1:1 (Hoffman and Baker, 2011). As in Sug_feed, surplus soybean meal and soybean oil are sold. Scenario Corn_past has the equivalent sugarcane, corn, and soybean areas, but without any integration.

The difference between Corn scenarios (Corn_feed and Corn_past) and Soy scenarios (Soy_feed Soy_past) is that the latter uses all soy- bean oil to produce biodiesel integrated with sugarcane ethanol, which is partially used in the transesterification (Olivério et al., 2014; Souza and Seabra, 2013, Scenario have an equiv- alent crop area, but only Soy_feed in feedlots.

1 Junqueira et al. (2016). 2 Moraes et al. (2014). 3 Bonomi et al. (2016). 4 Sampaio et al. (2019). 5 Souza et al. (2019). 6 Bonomi et al. (2019). 7 Olivério et al. (2014). 8 Dias et al. (2016). 9 Milanez et al. (2014). 10 Matsuura and Picoli (2018).

In all scenarios, sugarcane processing capacity is 4 million tonnes/ year, producing anhydrous ethanol and electricity. Electricity is pro- duced from sugarcane bagasse, straw and biogas produced from anaer- obic digestion of sugarcane vinasse (Moraes et al., 2014; Junqueira et al., 2016). Around 120 kg of straw is produced per tonne of sugarcane (Menandro et al., 2017) and 50% of it is recovered from the fields using bales system (Carvalho et al., 2017). At the mill, sugarcane straw is washed and crushed, reaching a 50% moisture (equally to bagasse), subsequently it is sent to the CHP unit where it is mixed with bagasse (Mantelatto et al., 2020). All scenarios include a plant to extract soybean oil, obtaining soymeal as coproduct. Both sugarcane and soybean pro- cessing plants operate 200 days per year.

Feed composition is based on Souza et al. (2019) and presented in Table S4 (Supplementary Material). To produce feed in the BLI scenar- ios, part of bagasse goes through a pretreatment process with steam ex- plosion to increase its nutritional value as beef cattle feed (Souza et al., 2019). The yeast from fermentation bleed is diverted to animal feed pro- duction. After meeting internal heat and power demands, sugarcane LCM can be diverted to feed, corn ethanol and biodiesel production. Sugarcane molasses, a by-product from sugar production, represents about 2% of feed composition and it is purchased from other sugarcane processing facilities with an annexed sugar production process.

Our assumptions for modelling the livestock system are simplified in face of the many specificities of the sector. For example, we consider a stabilized herd, with all animals at the same age and weight at the be- ginning and end of the finishing cycle. For conventional systems we consider an extensive management with a stocking rate of one head per hectare (EMBRAPA, 2014). In BLI scenarios, we assume that beef meat production would not be negatively affected by the crop's expan- sion, nor additional pasture area would be necessary. The number of

cattle heads is defined considering that for each hectare of released land for crop production, one cattle head should be fed with animal feed produced inside the system boundaries. The processing capacity of sugarcane plants define the sugarcane agricultural area and the corn processing capacity, consequently, corn/soybean agricultural area. In this way, cattle heads are equal to total hectares of crops production in BLI systems.

The key BLI aspects include: a) manure replacing part of mineral ni- trogen fertilizer (i.e. urea); b) sugarcane plant providing power and heat for soybean and corn processing plants; c) soybean extraction and anaerobic digestion plant operate during the 200 days of sugarcane season; d) corn ethanol plant operates during 130 days in sugarcane off- season; e) biogas from anaerobic digestion of vinasse is burnt to gener- ate electricity; f) yeast from sugarcane ethanol fermentation is used in the animal feed formulation; and g) ethanol is used for biodiesel transesterification. Although methanol is frequently used (EPE, 2019), we consider use of ethanol to maximize the integration, as in Olivério et al. (2014).
17: <header>2. Methodology </header>
2.1. Modelling and simulation of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems

The BLI scenarios are designed considering possible options for live- stock intensification and use of biofuels by-products (e.g., sugarcane ba- gasse, molasses, yeast, DGS, soybean meal) as animal feed supplement, in a land management perspective based on positive experiences in Brazil and in other countries, such as the United States. Unlike DGS and soybean meal traditionally used as animal feed, sugarcane by- products have different destinations. Sugarcane bagasse is currently used to generate steam and electricity in sugarcane biorefineries, molas- ses is used in ethanol production (Souza et al., 2021a), and yeasts, that are not recycled, normally goes to vinasse (Moraes et al., 2015), how- ever, they have nutritional value as animal feed (Souza et al., 2021a). The integration is carried out by expanding biofuel crops on released pasture area. This release of pasture area is only possible due to the use of biofuel by-products as cattle feed, that are fed on feedlots.

Simulations of BLI supply-chain are performed on the Virtual Biorefinery (VB) framework (Bonomi et al., 2016), developed by the Brazilian Biorenewables National Laboratory (LNBR) and adapted to the scope of this work (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). This frame- work allows simulating the whole supply-chain, from biomass produc- tion, logistics, and industrial operations to convert biomass into products. The agricultural stage is modeled in the VB using CanaSoft tool, an excel based series of spreadsheets considering all inputs and fuels used in all the agricultural operation, such as soil preparation, planting, harvesting, application of fertilizers, agrochemicals, and use of industrial residues in the field. Although developed initially for sugarcane, CanaSoft® has been customized to represent other crops, including corn and soybean. The industrial operations are modeled con- sidering mass and energy balances from VB sugarcane biorefineries, while additional operations for corn and soybean processing were based on literature (Table S1, Supplementary Material). The logistics in- cluded the transportation distances and consumption of fuels to deliver

inputs for agricultural stage of production, biomass for biorefinery, and residues back to the field. For this study, the finishing stage of beef cattle value chain and feed production on biorefineries are inserted and modeled on the VB using data based both in literature and exemplary BLI cases in real world. The life cycle inventories and inputs for cash flow analysis from agricultural and industrial modelling are used to as- sess techno-economic feasibility and environmental assessment using LCA methodology.

We considered the three main biofuel crops in the country. For in- stance, 90% of total 30 billion 1 of ethanol produced in Brazil is from sug- arcane, the remaining volume is produced from corn (CONAB, 2020). Total sugarcane production account for around 10 million ha (IBGE, 2021b), about half of it used for ethanol (CONAB, 2020). Total area of corn in Brazil is around 18 million ha (IBGE, 2021c). Around 74% of the 5 billion 1 of biodiesel produced in the country is from soybean oil (ANP, 2021) and total soybean area in the country is about 36 million ha (IBGE, 2021c).

We assessed seven scenarios, considering different levels of integra- tion with sugarcane, corn and soybean (Table 1). The first scenario is a base of comparison with beef cattle and sugarcane ethanol produced in conventional systems, without any integration (Base). Conventional scenarios include cattle finished in grass-fed pasture systems. BLI sce- narios consider cattle finished in feedlots with biorefineries producing feed ingredients. Feedlot area is considered neglectable because it typi- cally represents less than 1% of total study area. BLI scenarios consider crop expansion (sugarcane area, plus corn in rotation with soybean area) on released pasture area. Corn and soybean rotation happens by using the same area in the same year to plant and harvest both crops se- quentially in different periods of the year, a very common practice in Brazil (Magalhães et al., 2020). Main parameters used to model the assessed scenarios are presented in Table 2. Additional information about industrial conversion systems is presented in Table S2 (Supple- mentary Material).

Scenario Sug_feed considers a sugarcane plant integrated to beef cattle production and sugarcane by-products (i.e., bagasse and yeast) used as part of animal feed. Using bagasse as animal feed reduces elec- tricity production but using bleed yeasts has no impacts in the biorefinery. The necessary corn area in is calculated based on feed requirements. Soybean is produced in rotation with corn, and part of soybean meal produced in this area composes animal feed, while soybean oil and surplus meal are sold to the market. The equiva- lent crop area is considered in Sug_past, but the sugarcane plant does not produce any feed, therefore all soybean meal, soybean oil and corn grain are marketed.

In scenarios Corn_past and Corn_feed, corn ethanol plants are annexed to sugarcane plants and operate during sugarcane offseason (130 days), in the so-called "flex" ethanol plants. Corn ethanol plants use energy provided by extended operation of sugarcane combined heat and power generation (CHP) unit that runs with sugarcane ligno- cellulosic material (LCM) (i.e., bagasse and straw) during sugarcane off-season. Daily sugarcane ethanol production volume determines the corn ethanol plant size since they use the same distillation and de- hydration facilities. Corn processing capacity defines corn area. Corn ethanol is produced using dry-grind process. Ethanol is the main prod- uct, and the DGS co-products are dried, becoming DDGS, and are used as animal feed (USGC, 2012). In these two scenarios corn grain is replaced in the feed formulation by DDGS in a proportion of 1:1 (Hoffman and Baker, 2011). As in Sug_feed, surplus soybean meal and soybean oil are sold. Scenario Corn_past has the equivalent sugarcane, corn, and soybean areas, but without any integration.

The difference between Corn scenarios (Corn_feed and Corn_past) and Soy scenarios (Soy_feed Soy_past) is that the latter uses all soy- bean oil to produce biodiesel integrated with sugarcane ethanol, which is partially used in the transesterification (Olivério et al., 2014; Souza and Seabra, 2013, Scenario have an equiv- alent crop area, but only Soy_feed in feedlots.

The key BLI aspects include: a) manure replacing part of mineral ni- trogen fertilizer (i.e. urea); b) sugarcane plant providing power and heat for soybean and corn processing plants; c) soybean extraction and anaerobic digestion plant operate during the 200 days of sugarcane season; d) corn ethanol plant operates during 130 days in sugarcane off- season; e) biogas from anaerobic digestion of vinasse is burnt to gener- ate electricity; f) yeast from sugarcane ethanol fermentation is used in the animal feed formulation; and g) ethanol is used for biodiesel transesterification. Although methanol is frequently used (EPE, 2019), we consider use of ethanol to maximize the integration, as in Olivério et al. (2014).
19: <header>2. Methodology </header>
2.1. Modelling and simulation of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems

The BLI scenarios are designed considering possible options for live- stock intensification and use of biofuels by-products (e.g., sugarcane ba- gasse, molasses, yeast, DGS, soybean meal) as animal feed supplement, in a land management perspective based on positive experiences in Brazil and in other countries, such as the United States. Unlike DGS and soybean meal traditionally used as animal feed, sugarcane by- products have different destinations. Sugarcane bagasse is currently used to generate steam and electricity in sugarcane biorefineries, molas- ses is used in ethanol production (Souza et al., 2021a), and yeasts, that are not recycled, normally goes to vinasse (Moraes et al., 2015), how- ever, they have nutritional value as animal feed (Souza et al., 2021a). The integration is carried out by expanding biofuel crops on released pasture area. This release of pasture area is only possible due to the use of biofuel by-products as cattle feed, that are fed on feedlots.

Simulations of BLI supply-chain are performed on the Virtual Biorefinery (VB) framework (Bonomi et al., 2016), developed by the Brazilian Biorenewables National Laboratory (LNBR) and adapted to the scope of this work (Fig. S1, Supplementary Material). This frame- work allows simulating the whole supply-chain, from biomass produc- tion, logistics, and industrial operations to convert biomass into products. The agricultural stage is modeled in the VB using CanaSoft tool, an excel based series of spreadsheets considering all inputs and fuels used in all the agricultural operation, such as soil preparation, planting, harvesting, application of fertilizers, agrochemicals, and use of industrial residues in the field. Although developed initially for sugarcane, CanaSoft® has been customized to represent other crops, including corn and soybean. The industrial operations are modeled con- sidering mass and energy balances from VB sugarcane biorefineries, while additional operations for corn and soybean processing were based on literature (Table S1, Supplementary Material). The logistics in- cluded the transportation distances and consumption of fuels to deliver

inputs for agricultural stage of production, biomass for biorefinery, and residues back to the field. For this study, the finishing stage of beef cattle value chain and feed production on biorefineries are inserted and modeled on the VB using data based both in literature and exemplary BLI cases in real world. The life cycle inventories and inputs for cash flow analysis from agricultural and industrial modelling are used to as- sess techno-economic feasibility and environmental assessment using LCA methodology.

We considered the three main biofuel crops in the country. For in- stance, 90% of total 30 billion 1 of ethanol produced in Brazil is from sug- arcane, the remaining volume is produced from corn (CONAB, 2020). Total sugarcane production account for around 10 million ha (IBGE, 2021b), about half of it used for ethanol (CONAB, 2020). Total area of corn in Brazil is around 18 million ha (IBGE, 2021c). Around 74% of the 5 billion 1 of biodiesel produced in the country is from soybean oil (ANP, 2021) and total soybean area in the country is about 36 million ha (IBGE, 2021c).

We assessed seven scenarios, considering different levels of integra- tion with sugarcane, corn and soybean (Table 1). The first scenario is a base of comparison with beef cattle and sugarcane ethanol produced in conventional systems, without any integration (Base). Conventional scenarios include cattle finished in grass-fed pasture systems. BLI sce- narios consider cattle finished in feedlots with biorefineries producing feed ingredients. Feedlot area is considered neglectable because it typi- cally represents less than 1% of total study area. BLI scenarios consider crop expansion (sugarcane area, plus corn in rotation with soybean area) on released pasture area. Corn and soybean rotation happens by using the same area in the same year to plant and harvest both crops se- quentially in different periods of the year, a very common practice in Brazil (Magalhães et al., 2020). Main parameters used to model the assessed scenarios are presented in Table 2. Additional information about industrial conversion systems is presented in Table S2 (Supple- mentary Material).

Scenario Sug_feed considers a sugarcane plant integrated to beef cattle production and sugarcane by-products (i.e., bagasse and yeast) used as part of animal feed. Using bagasse as animal feed reduces elec- tricity production but using bleed yeasts has no impacts in the biorefinery. The necessary corn area in is calculated based on feed requirements. Soybean is produced in rotation with corn, and part of soybean meal produced in this area composes animal feed, while soybean oil and surplus meal are sold to the market. The equiva- lent crop area is considered in Sug_past, but the sugarcane plant does not produce any feed, therefore all soybean meal, soybean oil and corn grain are marketed.

In scenarios Corn_past and Corn_feed, corn ethanol plants are annexed to sugarcane plants and operate during sugarcane offseason (130 days), in the so-called "flex" ethanol plants. Corn ethanol plants use energy provided by extended operation of sugarcane combined heat and power generation (CHP) unit that runs with sugarcane ligno- cellulosic material (LCM) (i.e., bagasse and straw) during sugarcane off-season. Daily sugarcane ethanol production volume determines the corn ethanol plant size since they use the same distillation and de- hydration facilities. Corn processing capacity defines corn area. Corn ethanol is produced using dry-grind process. Ethanol is the main prod- uct, and the DGS co-products are dried, becoming DDGS, and are used as animal feed (USGC, 2012). In these two scenarios corn grain is replaced in the feed formulation by DDGS in a proportion of 1:1 (Hoffman and Baker, 2011). As in Sug_feed, surplus soybean meal and soybean oil are sold. Scenario Corn_past has the equivalent sugarcane, corn, and soybean areas, but without any integration.

The difference between Corn scenarios (Corn_feed and Corn_past) and Soy scenarios (Soy_feed Soy_past) is that the latter uses all soy- bean oil to produce biodiesel integrated with sugarcane ethanol, which is partially used in the transesterification (Olivério et al., 2014; Souza and Seabra, 2013, Scenario have an equiv- alent crop area, but only Soy_feed in feedlots.

1 Junqueira et al. (2016). 2 Moraes et al. (2014). 3 Bonomi et al. (2016). 4 Sampaio et al. (2019). 5 Souza et al. (2019). 6 Bonomi et al. (2019). 7 Olivério et al. (2014). 8 Dias et al. (2016). 9 Milanez et al. (2014). 10 Matsuura and Picoli (2018).

In all scenarios, sugarcane processing capacity is 4 million tonnes/ year, producing anhydrous ethanol and electricity. Electricity is pro- duced from sugarcane bagasse, straw and biogas produced from anaer- obic digestion of sugarcane vinasse (Moraes et al., 2014; Junqueira et al., 2016). Around 120 kg of straw is produced per tonne of sugarcane (Menandro et al., 2017) and 50% of it is recovered from the fields using bales system (Carvalho et al., 2017). At the mill, sugarcane straw is washed and crushed, reaching a 50% moisture (equally to bagasse), subsequently it is sent to the CHP unit where it is mixed with bagasse (Mantelatto et al., 2020). All scenarios include a plant to extract soybean oil, obtaining soymeal as coproduct. Both sugarcane and soybean pro- cessing plants operate 200 days per year.

Feed composition is based on Souza et al. (2019) and presented in Table S4 (Supplementary Material). To produce feed in the BLI scenar- ios, part of bagasse goes through a pretreatment process with steam ex- plosion to increase its nutritional value as beef cattle feed (Souza et al., 2019). The yeast from fermentation bleed is diverted to animal feed pro- duction. After meeting internal heat and power demands, sugarcane LCM can be diverted to feed, corn ethanol and biodiesel production. Sugarcane molasses, a by-product from sugar production, represents about 2% of feed composition and it is purchased from other sugarcane processing facilities with an annexed sugar production process.

Our assumptions for modelling the livestock system are simplified in face of the many specificities of the sector. For example, we consider a stabilized herd, with all animals at the same age and weight at the be- ginning and end of the finishing cycle. For conventional systems we consider an extensive management with a stocking rate of one head per hectare (EMBRAPA, 2014). In BLI scenarios, we assume that beef meat production would not be negatively affected by the crop's expan- sion, nor additional pasture area would be necessary. The number of

Conventional scenarios include cattle finished in grass-fed pasture systems. BLI sce- narios consider cattle finished in feedlots with biorefineries producing feed ingredients. Feedlot area is considered neglectable because it typi- cally represents less than 1% of total study area. BLI scenarios consider crop expansion (sugarcane area, plus corn in rotation with soybean area) on released pasture area. Corn and soybean rotation happens by using the same area in the same year to plant and harvest both crops se- quentially in different periods of the year, a very common practice in Brazil (Magalhães et al., 2020). Main parameters used to model the assessed scenarios are presented in Table 2. Additional information about industrial conversion systems is presented in Table S2 (Supple- mentary Material).

Scenario Sug_feed considers a sugarcane plant integrated to beef cattle production and sugarcane by-products (i.e., bagasse and yeast) used as part of animal feed. Using bagasse as animal feed reduces elec- tricity production but using bleed yeasts has no impacts in the biorefinery. The necessary corn area in is calculated based on feed requirements. Soybean is produced in rotation with corn, and part of soybean meal produced in this area composes animal feed, while soybean oil and surplus meal are sold to the market. The equiva- lent crop area is considered in Sug_past, but the sugarcane plant does not produce any feed, therefore all soybean meal, soybean oil and corn grain are marketed.

In scenarios Corn_past and Corn_feed, corn ethanol plants are annexed to sugarcane plants and operate during sugarcane offseason (130 days), in the so-called "flex" ethanol plants. Corn ethanol plants use energy provided by extended operation of sugarcane combined heat and power generation (CHP) unit that runs with sugarcane ligno- cellulosic material (LCM) (i.e., bagasse and straw) during sugarcane off-season. Daily sugarcane ethanol production volume determines the corn ethanol plant size since they use the same distillation and de- hydration facilities. Corn processing capacity defines corn area. Corn ethanol is produced using dry-grind process. Ethanol is the main prod- uct, and the DGS co-products are dried, becoming DDGS, and are used as animal feed (USGC, 2012). In these two scenarios corn grain is replaced in the feed formulation by DDGS in a proportion of 1:1 (Hoffman and Baker, 2011). As in Sug_feed, surplus soybean meal and soybean oil are sold. Scenario Corn_past has the equivalent sugarcane, corn, and soybean areas, but without any integration.

The difference between Corn scenarios (Corn_feed and Corn_past) and Soy scenarios (Soy_feed Soy_past) is that the latter uses all soy- bean oil to produce biodiesel integrated with sugarcane ethanol, which is partially used in the transesterification (Olivério et al., 2014; Souza and Seabra, 2013, Scenario have an equiv- alent crop area, but only Soy_feed in feedlots.

agricultural phase of all BLI scenarios (Sug_feed, Corn_feed and Soy_feed), manure from cattle on feedlots is applied in sugarcane fields to replace part of the N fertilizer, according to the amount of manure produced in each scenario (Table S3), considering a 70% recovery of N content in manure. The agricultural stage inventories are presented in Table 3.

1 Junqueira et al. (2016). 2 Moraes et al. (2014). 3 Bonomi et al. (2016). 4 Sampaio et al. (2019). 5 Souza et al. (2019). 6 Bonomi et al. (2019). 7 Olivério et al. (2014). 8 Dias et al. (2016). 9 Milanez et al. (2014). 10 Matsuura and Picoli (2018).

In all scenarios, sugarcane processing capacity is 4 million tonnes/ year, producing anhydrous ethanol and electricity. Electricity is pro- duced from sugarcane bagasse, straw and biogas produced from anaer- obic digestion of sugarcane vinasse (Moraes et al., 2014; Junqueira et al., 2016). Around 120 kg of straw is produced per tonne of sugarcane (Menandro et al., 2017) and 50% of it is recovered from the fields using bales system (Carvalho et al., 2017). At the mill, sugarcane straw is washed and crushed, reaching a 50% moisture (equally to bagasse), subsequently it is sent to the CHP unit where it is mixed with bagasse (Mantelatto et al., 2020). All scenarios include a plant to extract soybean oil, obtaining soymeal as coproduct. Both sugarcane and soybean pro- cessing plants operate 200 days per year.

Feed composition is based on Souza et al. (2019) and presented in Table S4 (Supplementary Material). To produce feed in the BLI scenar- ios, part of bagasse goes through a pretreatment process with steam ex- plosion to increase its nutritional value as beef cattle feed (Souza et al., 2019). The yeast from fermentation bleed is diverted to animal feed pro- duction. After meeting internal heat and power demands, sugarcane LCM can be diverted to feed, corn ethanol and biodiesel production. Sugarcane molasses, a by-product from sugar production, represents about 2% of feed composition and it is purchased from other sugarcane processing facilities with an annexed sugar production process.

Our assumptions for modelling the livestock system are simplified in face of the many specificities of the sector. For example, we consider a stabilized herd, with all animals at the same age and weight at the be- ginning and end of the finishing cycle. For conventional systems we consider an extensive management with a stocking rate of one head per hectare (EMBRAPA, 2014). In BLI scenarios, we assume that beef meat production would not be negatively affected by the crop's expan- sion, nor additional pasture area would be necessary. The number of
