cattle heads is defined considering that for each hectare of released land for crop production, one cattle head should be fed with animal feed produced inside the system boundaries. The processing capacity of sugarcane plants define the sugarcane agricultural area and the corn processing capacity, consequently, corn/soybean agricultural area. In this way, cattle heads are equal to total hectares of crops production in BLI systems.

The key BLI aspects include: a) manure replacing part of mineral ni- trogen fertilizer (i.e. urea); b) sugarcane plant providing power and heat for soybean and corn processing plants; c) soybean extraction and anaerobic digestion plant operate during the 200 days of sugarcane season; d) corn ethanol plant operates during 130 days in sugarcane off- season; e) biogas from anaerobic digestion of vinasse is burnt to gener- ate electricity; f) yeast from sugarcane ethanol fermentation is used in the animal feed formulation; and g) ethanol is used for biodiesel transesterification. Although methanol is frequently used (EPE, 2019), we consider use of ethanol to maximize the integration, as in Olivério et al. (2014).
22: Life cycle assessment is built on a life cycle thinking perspective to provide a standard method used for systematization, quantification and evaluation of the inputs, outputs, and potential environmental im- pacts throughout the life-cycle of a product, process or service. The method includes use of resources and emissions from raw material ac- quisition through production, use, end-of-life treatment, recycling and final disposal (Hellweg et al., 2014, ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006).

The goal of this work is to assess the environmental implications of BLI compared to conventional systems considering selected environ- mental impact categories from ReCiPe 2016 life cycle impact assess- ment method (Huijbregts et al., 2017). Our LCA considers a cradle-to- gate approach for seven scenarios described in Section 2.1, that produce biofuels and meat in both conventional and BLI systems. The simplified product system for both cases is shown in Fig. 1. Cow-calf system is not considered in the scope of this study, but calves' life-cycle impacts are taken into account as an input to the BLI systems. The functional units 1 MJ of biofuel (i.e., ethanol and biodiesel) and 1 kg of meat were

selected as reference flows to compare results. We also addressed BLI impacts towards Sustainable Development Goals, following the meth- odology for LCA results interpretation under an SDG context proposed by Cavalett and Cherubini (2018) and Souza et al. (2022) (Table S5, from Supplementary material).

In our approach, subdivision of the processes is applied whenever possible to avoid allocation. When subdivision was not possible, we used energy allocation (e.g., sugarcane electricity, ethanol; corn ethanol, oil and DDGS; soybean oil, soymeal, glycerin, and biodiesel), in line with the directives used in the Brazilian national biofuel policy, named RenovaBio program (Matsuura et al., 2018). This program establishes decarbonization targets for liquid fuels used in the transport sector and provides economic benefits for biofuel producers based on their carbon footprint in comparison to fossil fuels. Values for energy alloca- tion are presented in Table S6 from Supplementary Material. Some in- termediate flows (e.g., feed components and sugarcane electricity being used by corn and soybean plants) carry energy allocated environ- mental impacts in their respective subsystems. An illustrative represen- tation of the subdivided systems and intermediate flows from the biorefinery perspective is presented in Fig. 2 for the most complex BLI scenario (i.e., Soy_feed).

Life cycle inventories (LCI) considering use of inputs and emissions to air, soil and water are derived from a modelling approach using the VB framework. Biofuel inventories consider biomass production, trans- portation to the biorefinery and industrial conversion stages. LCI for finishing stage of beef cattle production systems include emissions from enteric fermentation and manure management and were calcu- lated according to IPCC (2006) guidelines and Matsuura and Picoli (2018). In the case of pasture management in scenarios without inte- gration, we included emissions for lime and urea application (IPCC, 2006). A detailed description is presented in Section 1.1 and Table S7 from Supplementary material.

Carbon emission or removals from land use change (LUC) emissions were not included in the main results, as the LUC from pasture to peren- nial or annual crops are site specific and strongly dependent of model- ling assumptions. These impacts are included within the sensitivity analysis in Section 3.3 using different sets of approaches. Avoided GHG emissions are calculated comparing biofuels with their fossil equivalent.
Fig. 1. Simplified product system for conventional (a) and BLI systems (b).

Fig. 2. Subdivision of BLI in four sub-systems for the life cycle analysis a) sugarcane processing plant; b) feed production, c) corn processing plant, and d) soybean processing plant. Dashed arrows represent intermediate flows carrying impacts between different sub-systems.
For ethanol, gasoline with carbon intensity of 87.4 gCO2eq/MJ is used, and for biodiesel, diesel with carbon intensity of 86.5 gCO2eq/MJ (MME, 2018) is used.

It considers a lin- ear depreciation in 10 years period, 25 years of expected lifetime and a minimum rate of return of 12% per year (Watanabe et al., 2016). Main economic parameters are presented in Table 4 and additional
28: Item|Value|Unit Expected plant lifetime|25|Years Minimum acceptable rate of return (per year)|12|% per year Depreciation|10|Linear years US dollar exchange rate8|4.11|R$/USD dec-2019 Prices|Prices|Prices Anhydrous ethanol2|0.47|USD/L Electricity3|51.37|USD/MWh Soybean meal4|0.40|USD/kg Soybean oil4|0.66|USD/kg Biodiesel4|0.86|USD/kg Glycerin4|0.54|USD/kg DDGS5,6|0.17|USD/kg Corn oil4|0.67|USD/kg Cattle, live weight7|2.40|USD/kg

information is provided in Table S8 (Supplementary Material). Eco- nomic inventories are calculated using the VB framework. We consid- ered that biomass, biofuel and livestock enterprises are the same project, in a vertical approach.

The economic parameters selected for comparison are payback time and ratio of Net Present Value (NPV) to initial investment. We also cal- culated the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) for all BLI and conventional scenarios. These economic parameters rely on cash flow analysis, which depends on data collection of capital expenditures - CAPEX (in- vestment in buildings, equipment, land, herd, working capital, etc.); on revenues (based on market prices of products such as ethanol, sugar, electricity, beef cattle, and others); and on operating costs - OPEX (expenditures associated with feedstock, labor, maintenance, chemicals, utilities, feed, etc.). Feed costs are estimated based on costs of ingredients produced internally in the BLI scenarios (corn grain, soy- bean meal, yeast, bagasse in natura and hydrolyzed, DDGS) and external market prices for mineral salt, molasses, yeast, and urea. For yeast and bagasse, we consider opportunity costs based on the sugarcane price calculated by CanaSoft®. Agricultural costs include inputs purchase and transportation, fuel consumption, machinery, labor, and deprecia- tion, among others. Total agricultural and industrial costs of corn pro- duction are allocated to DDGS accordingly to its production share. The same approach is applied to calculate soybean meal cost considering soybean plant.

information is provided in Table S8 (Supplementary Material). Eco- nomic inventories are calculated using the VB framework. We consid- ered that biomass, biofuel and livestock enterprises are the same project, in a vertical approach.

The economic parameters selected for comparison are payback time and ratio of Net Present Value (NPV) to initial investment. We also cal- culated the Internal Rate of Return (IRR) for all BLI and conventional scenarios. These economic parameters rely on cash flow analysis, which depends on data collection of capital expenditures - CAPEX (in- vestment in buildings, equipment, land, herd, working capital, etc.); on revenues (based on market prices of products such as ethanol, sugar, electricity, beef cattle, and others); and on operating costs - OPEX (expenditures associated with feedstock, labor, maintenance, chemicals, utilities, feed, etc.). Feed costs are estimated based on costs of ingredients produced internally in the BLI scenarios (corn grain, soy- bean meal, yeast, bagasse in natura and hydrolyzed, DDGS) and external market prices for mineral salt, molasses, yeast, and urea. For yeast and bagasse, we consider opportunity costs based on the sugarcane price calculated by CanaSoft®. Agricultural costs include inputs purchase and transportation, fuel consumption, machinery, labor, and deprecia- tion, among others. Total agricultural and industrial costs of corn pro- duction are allocated to DDGS accordingly to its production share. The same approach is applied to calculate soybean meal cost considering soybean plant.

31: 3.1. Economic feasibility and avoided GHG emissions of BLI and conventional systems

To assess the potential advantages of BLI systems when compared to conventional systems, we performed a techno-economic and environ- mental assessment of the proposed scenarios. As result, Sug feed sce- nario presented the highest potential to avoid GHG emissions per hectare (Fig. 3a), that is 2 times higher than conventional systems
Fig. 3. Comparison of BLI and conventional scenarios for (a) avoided GHG emission per land used, and (b) economic feasibility.

(Base scenario). This is mostly due to higher sugarcane biofuel yields when compared to corn and soybean, resulting in 5378 kg of sugarcane ethanol per hectare, against 1740 kg of corn ethanol per hectare and 620 kg of biodiesel per hectare, respectively. Also, corn and soybean GHG emissions are much higher than sugarcane, being 253.2 kgCO2eq/t of corn and 161.8 kgCO2eq/t of soybean, against 39.6 kgCO2eq/t of sugarcane (Table S9, Supplementary Material). Biomass production contributed to most of GHG emissions for biofuel production, ranging from 80% to 95% of total emissions. When comparing BLI scenarios with their respective conventional systems, integration increased avoided GHG emissions up to 60% (Table S9, Supplementary Material). It happens mostly because there are more outputs using less land and there are lower livestock emissions, that can be explained by a shorter life cycle comparing feedlots (120 days) with pasture (365 days), no emissions associated with pasture management (i.e., soil correctives, transportation) and smaller area to finish cattle.

In a techno-economic perspective, scenarios Corn_feed and Soy_feed presented the highest feasibility among all scenarios (Fig. 3b), with only 3.1 years for payback time and a 1.2 ratio of NPV/in- vestment, showing that their integration improved overall techno- economic feasibility. It happens mainly due to higher revenues from corn and soybean products with low investments since they use part of the idle sugarcane processing facilities (Table S10). Scenarios Corn_feed and Soy_feed presented basically the same IRR, but the latter presented a NPV that was 16 M USD larger due to a bigger gross reve- nue. Although total CAPEX and OPEX are higher in scenarios with corn ethanol and biodiesel production, these are balanced with larger reve- nues from their associated products, with high market prices (Figs. S2

and S3, Supplementary Material). Compared to conventional system (base scenario) scenarios Corn_feed and Soy_feed resulted in a 43% lower payback time, a 5-fold increase in ratio of NPV to initial invest- ments, and an IRR 10 percentage points higher.

In general, BLI scenarios were more economically feasible due to lower CAPEX and OPEX compared to conventional systems. The lower OPEX of BLI systems can be attributed to the fact that cattle in feedlots do not require agricultural inputs or land costs, as well as decreased transportation, fuel consumption, and maintenance expenses. Total in- vestments are 6% larger in conventional scenarios because cattle in feedlot require less investments and when no feed is produced, the CHP requires slightly larger equipment (see Fig. S4 in the Supplemen- tary Material). Conversely, feed production represented less than 1% of total investments in all assessed scenarios.

A breakdown of biorefinery investments (Fig. S4) revealed that sug- arcane plant accounted for highest investments in all scenarios, from about 38% to 55%. In scenarios without corn ethanol production, CHP unit had the second largest investment costs, that ranged from 38% to 41%. Investments for the corn ethanol plant represented the second largest shares in scenarios with corn ethanol production, ranging from 28 to 30%, considerably high, since part of sugarcane equipment is used by the corn plant during offseason. Anaerobic biodigestion of vinasse accounts for 5-7% of total biorefinery investments. In scenarios processing only sugarcane, soybean oil extraction is only for feed pro- duction, which explains the relatively low share (1%) compared to scenarios where biofuels are produced with corn and soybean (Corn_feed, orn_past, Soy_feed,Soy_past) In terms of costs, slight dif- ferences on sugarcane costs (i.e., 2%) in BLI scenarios (Table S12 from

Supplementary Material) can be explained by different urea application, since scenarios Corn_feed and Soy_feed have higher soybean and corn areas, consequently more cattle on feedlots and more manure available to replace urea applied on sugarcane fields. These larger areas also ex- plain why these crops are 3% more expensive than in Feed costs varied slightly in the three scenarios with integration due to differ- ent by-products costs (Fig. S13, Supplementary material). A breakdown of GHG emissions for sugarcane ethanol and meat, common outputs of all scenarios, showed that sugarcane ethanol pro- duced in BLI systems reduced GHG emissions up to 20% compared to conventional systems (Table S14, Supplementary material). Lower GHG emissions of sugarcane ethanol in integrated scenarios are mainly related to allocation to feed ingredients and lower emissions from CHP due to bagasse use as feed ingredient. Most of sugarcane ethanol emis- sions are caused by fertilizer production and its application on the field (Fig. S5a, Supplementary material). Before allocation, integrated scenar- ios presented slightly lower emissions than conventional systems be- cause part of bagasse is used as animal feed instead of burnt for energy purposes (Fig. S6, Supplementary material). As in the agricul- tural costs, lower amounts of manure replacing urea on sugarcane fields in Sug_feed explain the slight differences on GHG emissions compared to Corn_feed and Soy_feed. Although manure application on the fields also promotes GHG emissions, they are relatively lower than urea pro- duction, transportation, and use. Even with additional GHG emissions from feed production, GHG emissions from meat production were re- duced by 23% when cattle were finished in feedlots mostly due to re- duced slaughter time that decreased enteric fermentation and manure handling emissions, and no emissions associated with pasture manage- ment (i.e., soil correctives, transportation, etc.) (see Fig. S5b and Table S14). It is worthwhile mentioning that most of GHG emissions as- sociated to meat are related to calf production, which is the same in all scenarios (i.e., 11.9 gCO2eq/kg of meat, in live weight). Methane emissions (i.e., enteric fermentation) are reduced from 148 gCH4/kg meat LW in conventional systems to around 28 gCH4/kg meat LW in BLI, representing 81% reduction. For GHG emissions of all biomasses, and outputs, including main products and by-products, please see Table S9 in the Supplementary Material. For the outputs of corn and soy- bean plants, smaller differences in GHG emissions can be explained by lower carbon intensities of sugarcane electricity, due to allocation for feed ingredients in the sugarcane plant. A more detailed look into main outputs and by-products of BLI sys- tems (Table S15 in the Supplementary material), showed that scenario has the highest biofuel production among all scenarios, with total 259 million tonnes (Mt) of sugarcane ethanol, 175 Mt of corn eth- anol and 62 Mt of biodiesel, compared to 269 Mt of sugarcane ethanol in Base scenario. The produced ethanol is the same in all scenarios, how- ever, the considered output that can be sold is smaller in scenarios Soy_past and Soy_feed, because around 4% of it is diverted to biodiesel production. However, conventional scenarios without extended plant operation (Base and Sug_past) presented the higher outputs of surplus electricity among all scenarios of 694 TWh per year. Surplus electricity accounts for electricity produced during sugarcane season and offseason, when applicable, after meeting the internal energy and feed demands. Part of bagasse is used to produce feed when there is integra- tion, and less LCM material is sent to the CHP. In Sug_feed, nearly 7% of total bagasse is used for feed production plus 1% of LCM is burnt in boilers to supply steam to the pretreatment step for feed purposes, it led to an 8% difference in surplus electricity compared to past. In Corn_feed and Soy_feed, more cattle heads are finished in feedlots, thus 20% sugarcane bagasse is diverted to feed production and 3% of total LCM is burnt in the CHP due to pretreatment of bagasse for feed, additionally 28% of total LCM in Corn_feed and Soy_feed, and 24% in and Soy_past are stored to run the CHP during 130 days of sugarcane offseason. These LCM demands resulted in up to 44% reduc- tion of surplus electricity when compared to scenarios with only sugar- cane (i.e., Sug_feed). LCM demand to biodiesel production

reduced surplus electricity in only about 1% when comparing scenarios with and without biodiesel (Corn_feed and Corn_pasture with Soy_feed and Soy_pasture, respectively). Finally, as a BLI assessment assumption, the same amount of meat is produced whether in pasture or in feedlot system, however in the latter, less land is necessary. By BLI scenarios definition, all cattle heads from released pasture- land should be fed with feed produced in the integration area. Produced soybean meal and DDGS was enough to meet the nutritional demand of cattle in feedlots in all BLI scenarios and no external purchase was re- quired. There was a 75% surplus of soybean meal in Sug_feed, and 6% in Corn_feed and Soy_feed. In both and Soy_feed there was about 50% surplus of DDGs. All surplus by-products in BLI systems and total by-products from conventional scenarios are considered to be sold to the market. It is also worthwhile mentioning that soybean oil is used to produce biodiesel in scenarios Soy_past and Soy_feed, and corn oil is always sold in all scenarios that include corn ethanol produc- tion. Conversely, in scenarios Soy_feed it was necessary to purchase external yeast, since the amount provided by sugarcane plant was not enough to supply total feed demand.

To assess the potential advantages of BLI systems when compared to conventional systems, we performed a techno-economic and environ- mental assessment of the proposed scenarios. As result, Sug feed sce- nario presented the highest potential to avoid GHG emissions per hectare (Fig. 3a), that is 2 times higher than conventional systems
Fig. 3. Comparison of BLI and conventional scenarios for (a) avoided GHG emission per land used, and (b) economic feasibility.

(Base scenario). This is mostly due to higher sugarcane biofuel yields when compared to corn and soybean, resulting in 5378 kg of sugarcane ethanol per hectare, against 1740 kg of corn ethanol per hectare and 620 kg of biodiesel per hectare, respectively. Also, corn and soybean GHG emissions are much higher than sugarcane, being 253.2 kgCO2eq/t of corn and 161.8 kgCO2eq/t of soybean, against 39.6 kgCO2eq/t of sugarcane (Table S9, Supplementary Material). Biomass production contributed to most of GHG emissions for biofuel production, ranging from 80% to 95% of total emissions. When comparing BLI scenarios with their respective conventional systems, integration increased avoided GHG emissions up to 60% (Table S9, Supplementary Material). It happens mostly because there are more outputs using less land and there are lower livestock emissions, that can be explained by a shorter life cycle comparing feedlots (120 days) with pasture (365 days), no emissions associated with pasture management (i.e., soil correctives, transportation) and smaller area to finish cattle.

In a techno-economic perspective, scenarios Corn_feed and Soy_feed presented the highest feasibility among all scenarios (Fig. 3b), with only 3.1 years for payback time and a 1.2 ratio of NPV/in- vestment, showing that their integration improved overall techno- economic feasibility. It happens mainly due to higher revenues from corn and soybean products with low investments since they use part of the idle sugarcane processing facilities (Table S10). Scenarios Corn_feed and Soy_feed presented basically the same IRR, but the latter presented a NPV that was 16 M USD larger due to a bigger gross reve- nue. Although total CAPEX and OPEX are higher in scenarios with corn ethanol and biodiesel production, these are balanced with larger reve- nues from their associated products, with high market prices (Figs. S2

and S3, Supplementary Material). Compared to conventional system (base scenario) scenarios Corn_feed and Soy_feed resulted in a 43% lower payback time, a 5-fold increase in ratio of NPV to initial invest- ments, and an IRR 10 percentage points higher.

In general, BLI scenarios were more economically feasible due to lower CAPEX and OPEX compared to conventional systems. The lower OPEX of BLI systems can be attributed to the fact that cattle in feedlots do not require agricultural inputs or land costs, as well as decreased transportation, fuel consumption, and maintenance expenses. Total in- vestments are 6% larger in conventional scenarios because cattle in feedlot require less investments and when no feed is produced, the CHP requires slightly larger equipment (see Fig. S4 in the Supplemen- tary Material). Conversely, feed production represented less than 1% of total investments in all assessed scenarios.

A breakdown of biorefinery investments (Fig. S4) revealed that sug- arcane plant accounted for highest investments in all scenarios, from about 38% to 55%. In scenarios without corn ethanol production, CHP unit had the second largest investment costs, that ranged from 38% to 41%. Investments for the corn ethanol plant represented the second largest shares in scenarios with corn ethanol production, ranging from 28 to 30%, considerably high, since part of sugarcane equipment is used by the corn plant during offseason. Anaerobic biodigestion of vinasse accounts for 5-7% of total biorefinery investments. In scenarios processing only sugarcane, soybean oil extraction is only for feed pro- duction, which explains the relatively low share (1%) compared to scenarios where biofuels are produced with corn and soybean (Corn_feed, orn_past, Soy_feed,Soy_past) In terms of costs, slight dif- ferences on sugarcane costs (i.e., 2%) in BLI scenarios (Table S12 from

Supplementary Material) can be explained by different urea application, since scenarios Corn_feed and Soy_feed have higher soybean and corn areas, consequently more cattle on feedlots and more manure available to replace urea applied on sugarcane fields. These larger areas also ex- plain why these crops are 3% more expensive than in Feed costs varied slightly in the three scenarios with integration due to differ- ent by-products costs (Fig. S13, Supplementary material). A breakdown of GHG emissions for sugarcane ethanol and meat, common outputs of all scenarios, showed that sugarcane ethanol pro- duced in BLI systems reduced GHG emissions up to 20% compared to conventional systems (Table S14, Supplementary material). Lower GHG emissions of sugarcane ethanol in integrated scenarios are mainly related to allocation to feed ingredients and lower emissions from CHP due to bagasse use as feed ingredient. Most of sugarcane ethanol emis- sions are caused by fertilizer production and its application on the field (Fig. S5a, Supplementary material). Before allocation, integrated scenar- ios presented slightly lower emissions than conventional systems be- cause part of bagasse is used as animal feed instead of burnt for energy purposes (Fig. S6, Supplementary material). As in the agricul- tural costs, lower amounts of manure replacing urea on sugarcane fields in Sug_feed explain the slight differences on GHG emissions compared to Corn_feed and Soy_feed. Although manure application on the fields also promotes GHG emissions, they are relatively lower than urea pro- duction, transportation, and use. Even with additional GHG emissions from feed production, GHG emissions from meat production were re- duced by 23% when cattle were finished in feedlots mostly due to re- duced slaughter time that decreased enteric fermentation and manure handling emissions, and no emissions associated with pasture manage- ment (i.e., soil correctives, transportation, etc.) (see Fig. S5b and Table S14). It is worthwhile mentioning that most of GHG emissions as- sociated to meat are related to calf production, which is the same in all scenarios (i.e., 11.9 gCO2eq/kg of meat, in live weight). Methane emissions (i.e., enteric fermentation) are reduced from 148 gCH4/kg meat LW in conventional systems to around 28 gCH4/kg meat LW in BLI, representing 81% reduction. For GHG emissions of all biomasses, and outputs, including main products and by-products, please see Table S9 in the Supplementary Material. For the outputs of corn and soy- bean plants, smaller differences in GHG emissions can be explained by lower carbon intensities of sugarcane electricity, due to allocation for feed ingredients in the sugarcane plant. A more detailed look into main outputs and by-products of BLI sys- tems (Table S15 in the Supplementary material), showed that scenario has the highest biofuel production among all scenarios, with total 259 million tonnes (Mt) of sugarcane ethanol, 175 Mt of corn eth- anol and 62 Mt of biodiesel, compared to 269 Mt of sugarcane ethanol in Base scenario. The produced ethanol is the same in all scenarios, how- ever, the considered output that can be sold is smaller in scenarios Soy_past and Soy_feed, because around 4% of it is diverted to biodiesel production. However, conventional scenarios without extended plant operation (Base and Sug_past) presented the higher outputs of surplus electricity among all scenarios of 694 TWh per year. Surplus electricity accounts for electricity produced during sugarcane season and offseason, when applicable, after meeting the internal energy and feed demands. Part of bagasse is used to produce feed when there is integra- tion, and less LCM material is sent to the CHP. In Sug_feed, nearly 7% of total bagasse is used for feed production plus 1% of LCM is burnt in boilers to supply steam to the pretreatment step for feed purposes, it led to an 8% difference in surplus electricity compared to past. In Corn_feed and Soy_feed, more cattle heads are finished in feedlots, thus 20% sugarcane bagasse is diverted to feed production and 3% of total LCM is burnt in the CHP due to pretreatment of bagasse for feed, additionally 28% of total LCM in Corn_feed and Soy_feed, and 24% in and Soy_past are stored to run the CHP during 130 days of sugarcane offseason. These LCM demands resulted in up to 44% reduc- tion of surplus electricity when compared to scenarios with only sugar- cane (i.e., Sug_feed). LCM demand to biodiesel production

reduced surplus electricity in only about 1% when comparing scenarios with and without biodiesel (Corn_feed and Corn_pasture with Soy_feed and Soy_pasture, respectively). Finally, as a BLI assessment assumption, the same amount of meat is produced whether in pasture or in feedlot system, however in the latter, less land is necessary. By BLI scenarios definition, all cattle heads from released pasture- land should be fed with feed produced in the integration area. Produced soybean meal and DDGS was enough to meet the nutritional demand of cattle in feedlots in all BLI scenarios and no external purchase was re- quired. There was a 75% surplus of soybean meal in Sug_feed, and 6% in Corn_feed and Soy_feed. In both and Soy_feed there was about 50% surplus of DDGs. All surplus by-products in BLI systems and total by-products from conventional scenarios are considered to be sold to the market. It is also worthwhile mentioning that soybean oil is used to produce biodiesel in scenarios Soy_past and Soy_feed, and corn oil is always sold in all scenarios that include corn ethanol produc- tion. Conversely, in scenarios Soy_feed it was necessary to purchase external yeast, since the amount provided by sugarcane plant was not enough to supply total feed demand.

These larger areas also ex- plain why these crops are 3% more expensive than in Feed costs varied slightly in the three scenarios with integration due to differ- ent by-products costs (Fig. S13, Supplementary material). A breakdown of GHG emissions for sugarcane ethanol and meat, common outputs of all scenarios, showed that sugarcane ethanol pro- duced in BLI systems reduced GHG emissions up to 20% compared to conventional systems (Table S14, Supplementary material). Lower GHG emissions of sugarcane ethanol in integrated scenarios are mainly related to allocation to feed ingredients and lower emissions from CHP due to bagasse use as feed ingredient. Most of sugarcane ethanol emis- sions are caused by fertilizer production and its application on the field (Fig. S5a, Supplementary material). Before allocation, integrated scenar- ios presented slightly lower emissions than conventional systems be- cause part of bagasse is used as animal feed instead of burnt for energy purposes (Fig. S6, Supplementary material). As in the agricul- tural costs, lower amounts of manure replacing urea on sugarcane fields in Sug_feed explain the slight differences on GHG emissions compared to Corn_feed and Soy_feed. Although manure application on the fields also promotes GHG emissions, they are relatively lower than urea pro- duction, transportation, and use. Even with additional GHG emissions from feed production, GHG emissions from meat production were re- duced by 23% when cattle were finished in feedlots mostly due to re- duced slaughter time that decreased enteric fermentation and manure handling emissions, and no emissions associated with pasture manage- ment (i.e., soil correctives, transportation, etc.) (see Fig. S5b and Table S14). It is worthwhile mentioning that most of GHG emissions as- sociated to meat are related to calf production, which is the same in all scenarios (i.e., 11.9 gCO2eq/kg of meat, in live weight). Methane emissions (i.e., enteric fermentation) are reduced from 148 gCH4/kg meat LW in conventional systems to around 28 gCH4/kg meat LW in BLI, representing 81% reduction. For GHG emissions of all biomasses, and outputs, including main products and by-products, please see Table S9 in the Supplementary Material. For the outputs of corn and soy- bean plants, smaller differences in GHG emissions can be explained by lower carbon intensities of sugarcane electricity, due to allocation for feed ingredients in the sugarcane plant. A more detailed look into main outputs and by-products of BLI sys- tems (Table S15 in the Supplementary material), showed that scenario has the highest biofuel production among all scenarios, with total 259 million tonnes (Mt) of sugarcane ethanol, 175 Mt of corn eth- anol and 62 Mt of biodiesel, compared to 269 Mt of sugarcane ethanol in Base scenario. The produced ethanol is the same in all scenarios, how- ever, the considered output that can be sold is smaller in scenarios Soy_past and Soy_feed, because around 4% of it is diverted to biodiesel production. However, conventional scenarios without extended plant operation (Base and Sug_past) presented the higher outputs of surplus electricity among all scenarios of 694 TWh per year. Surplus electricity accounts for electricity produced during sugarcane season and offseason, when applicable, after meeting the internal energy and feed demands. Part of bagasse is used to produce feed when there is integra- tion, and less LCM material is sent to the CHP. In Sug_feed, nearly 7% of total bagasse is used for feed production plus 1% of LCM is burnt in boilers to supply steam to the pretreatment step for feed purposes, it led to an 8% difference in surplus electricity compared to past. In Corn_feed and Soy_feed, more cattle heads are finished in feedlots, thus 20% sugarcane bagasse is diverted to feed production and 3% of total LCM is burnt in the CHP due to pretreatment of bagasse for feed, additionally 28% of total LCM in Corn_feed and Soy_feed, and 24% in and Soy_past are stored to run the CHP during 130 days of sugarcane offseason. These LCM demands resulted in up to 44% reduc- tion of surplus electricity when compared to scenarios with only sugar- cane (i.e., Sug_feed). LCM demand to biodiesel production

reduced surplus electricity in only about 1% when comparing scenarios with and without biodiesel (Corn_feed and Corn_pasture with Soy_feed and Soy_pasture, respectively). Finally, as a BLI assessment assumption, the same amount of meat is produced whether in pasture or in feedlot system, however in the latter, less land is necessary. By BLI scenarios definition, all cattle heads from released pasture- land should be fed with feed produced in the integration area. Produced soybean meal and DDGS was enough to meet the nutritional demand of cattle in feedlots in all BLI scenarios and no external purchase was re- quired. There was a 75% surplus of soybean meal in Sug_feed, and 6% in Corn_feed and Soy_feed. In both and Soy_feed there was about 50% surplus of DDGs. All surplus by-products in BLI systems and total by-products from conventional scenarios are considered to be sold to the market. It is also worthwhile mentioning that soybean oil is used to produce biodiesel in scenarios Soy_past and Soy_feed, and corn oil is always sold in all scenarios that include corn ethanol produc- tion. Conversely, in scenarios Soy_feed it was necessary to purchase external yeast, since the amount provided by sugarcane plant was not enough to supply total feed demand.
38: <header>3.2. Comparison of BLI and conventional systems towards SDGs </header>

We interpreted the LCA results of conventional and BLI systems under an SDGs context for sugarcane ethanol and meat, two common outputs in all scenarios (Fig. 4). The arithmetic mean of the results from the four conventional and three BLI scenarios are applied. This is justified as the differences among them were only about 1% to 4%. Abso- lute LCA values in each category are presented in Table S16 of the Supplementary Material.

For sugarcane ethanol, conventional scenarios presented higher im- pacts than BLI scenarios in eight out of nine addressed SDGs. In SDGs Zero Hunger and SDG 3 - Good Health and Well-Being, BLI systems pre- sented lower impacts, mostly due to allocation to more products. Higher LCM burnt in boilers in conventional scenarios contributed to 10% higher terrestrial acidification and 9% higher fine particular matter for- mation, which can negatively impact on sustainable agricultural pro- duction (SDG 2) and air pollution, possibly leading to respiratory diseases (SDG 3). Higher mineral N fertilizer (i.e., urea) use led to a 27% higher freshwater eutrophication in conventional scenarios and can negatively impact on freshwater quality (SDG 6 - Clean Water and Sanitation). In SDG 7 - Affordable and Clean Energy, conventional scenarios used 21% more non-renewable resources, resulting in a higher fossil resource scarcity due to higher urea production and transporta- tion. In SDG 11 - Sustainable Cities and Communities, conventional sce- narios performed 17% worst due to higher ozone formation during urea production, leading to potential negative impacts in air quality. Compar- ing mineral resource scarcity, the indicator for SDG 12 - Responsible Consumption and Production, the conventional scenario presented 21% higher impacts due to larger CHP units since no LCM is diverted for feed purposes. Sugarcane ethanol and meat presented about 17% and 23% lower GHG emissions, respectively, when compared to conven- tional systems, having a better performance in SDG 13 - Climate Action. This is mostly due to allocation of overall emissions to feed components. All cattle are finished in pasture management and 20% more land is OC- cupied in conventional systems, leading to higher impacts in SDG 15 Life On Land. However, this 20% accounts for land use to produce the in- puts used in each scenario, as defined by ReCiPe 2016 method. In terms of occupied land, BLI systems could produce the same products as con- ventional systems using about half area (Table S15 in the Supplemen- tary material), what could lead to an even better performance towards SDG 15. SDG 14 - Life Below Water is the only SDG where conventional systems performed better than BLI (8%), mostly because of higher N- related emissions from manure application on sugarcane fields that led to higher marine eutrophication.
Some tradeoffs are observed comparing livestock in conventional and BLI systems. From the nine SDGs assessed in this work, conventional

3.3. Sensitivity analysis: maximization of BLI techno-economic and environmental performance

To overcome possible uncertainties and limitations from the as- sumptions considered in this study, we performed a sensitivity analysis to understand how variation of key parameters could impact on techno-

economic and environmental performance of BLI systems. We selected seven key parameters for the sensitivity analysis based on their contri- bution to the overall results and their intrinsic uncertainty and variabil- ity. They are sugarcane yields, recovery of N content from manure applied in sugarcane field, cattle stocking rate capacity, carcass yield, av- erage daily gains in feedlot systems, total investments, and sugarcane
Stocking rate
N recovery
ADG
Total investment

bagasse for feed composition (Table S17 in the Supplementary mate- rial). Sugarcane yield and cattle stocking rate capacity are site specific, and a large variability is expected. Recovery of N content from manure, average daily gain, and carcass yield can vary depending on feed compo- sition and cattle management. Amount of bagasse for feed production can vary depending on market conditions that can benefit electricity generation, for example. Finally, as the total investments have great in- fluence on techno-economic performance, it was a key parameter to be included in this sensitivity assessment. The lower and upper bounds of these variations were placed from -50% to 50% in relation to the default values used in scenario Soy_feed, the case with the highest integration.

From the seven key parameters, variation of sugarcane yield contrib- uted the most to all outputs considered (Fig. 5). Bagasse content on feed, on the other hand, had almost no impact in five out of the six outputs considered, it only considerably impacted on surplus electricity sold to the grid, since more bagasse for feed means less LCM available for CHP. The same effect holds true for variable stocking rate, since more cattle heads per hectare means more bagasse for feed and less for CHP. Cattle stocking rate also impacted on total land use, as higher stocking rates means lower land demand. As expected, higher carcass yields produced more meat and reduced GHG emissions associated with it, however these parameters did not affect other outputs. Average daily gain (ADG) slightly impacted on GHG emissions of sugarcane eth- anol because lower feedlot duration allowed a higher production of electricity, and more emissions were allocated to it. When more elec- tricity is produced NPV/investments increases as well. As expected, var- iation in total investments had the highest impacts on NPV/investments ratio indicator. Recovery of N on manure application on the field had al- most no impact in all assessed outputs.

The climate change mitigation potential of BLI systems can be im- pacted when accounting for land use change (LUC) emissions of crops expansion on released pastureland. A sensitivity assessment was per- formed considering four set of approaches to account for soil carbon emission changes and to understand their impact on total GHG emis- sions per hectare in all BLI scenarios. The Approach 1 considers the de- fault guidelines of Directive 2009/28/ECLUC (EC, 2010); Approach 2 considers LUC emissions as calculated by Chagas et al. (2016), consider- ing that expansion of crops (i.e., sugarcane and corn/soybean) happens on degraded pastureland; Approach 3 is similar to Approach 2, but ex- pansion happens in severely degraded pastureland; Approach 4 con- siders LUC emissions only from sugarcane expansion on pasture calculated by Picoli (2017). Land use change emissions were calculated according to IPCC (2006) guidelines, applying the equations detailed in Table S18 Section 3.2 of Supplementary Material. As result, accounting for LUC emissions of sugarcane and soybean/corn expansion on pasture- land could increase total GHG emissions per hectare up to 47% in Soy_feed in Approach 1 and reduce them up to 19% in Approach 3 (Fig. depending on soil organic carbon (SOC) and pas- ture condition before biofuel expansion. Corn and soybean are annual crops and are reported to have a lower SOC than pasture in all LUC changes accounting approaches. Therefore, expansion of these crops in pasture area are likely to cause relatively higher LUC compared with sugarcane expansion. However, LUC emissions strongly depend on site-specific edaphoclimatic conditions of the current and previous land uses (Lal, 2010) and require a deeper and more detailed assess- ment to be performed in future work.

Under the considered conditions, BLI scenarios can be economically feasible but high economic uncertainties are associated with the inte- gration (Souza and Seabra, 2014). A minimum acceptable rate of return per year of 12% was considered and any NPV above 0 represents the eco- nomic feasibility, which happened in all scenarios. Furthermore, fast paybacks are desirable in any enterprise. However, this 12% is accept- able for the sugarcane sector in conventional production pathways (Watanabe et al., 2016). In order to explore possibilities to improve eco- nomic feasibility of BLI systems, we performed one sensitivity assess- ments to account for extra revenues from carbon credit generation

and one to account for benefits when replacing diesel with biodiesel. Avoided GHG emissions from BLI systems could generate extra reve- nues in terms of carbon credits. For example, each tonne of avoided GHG emissions is equivalent to one decarbonization credit unit (CBIO) in RenovaBio program (MME, n.d.), and its market price is considered as 8.4 USD (B3, 2021). Although RenovaBio currently considers only CBIOs from liquid biofuels used in transport sector, we calculated possi- ble avoided GHG emissions also from cattle and electricity production as an indication for the monetization of their climate benefits. For cattle, the carbon intensity of meat in pasture is compared to meat in feedlot, calculated in this study; sugarcane electricity is compared with carbon intensity of natural gas electricity of 500 gCO2eq/kWh (Ecoinvent, 2018). Accounting for CBIOs generation from biofuels would increase overall revenues up to 14 M USD in Soy_fee (Fig. S8 in the Supplemen- tary Material). From the 9 million USD/year of Soy_feed considering only CBIO-biofuels, sugarcane electricity and meat could contribute to an additional 1.6 and 2.7 million USD, respectively, totalizing 13.38 mil- lion USD of CBIO revenues. The sugarcane related CBIOs increased 2 to 3 million USD per year when considering additional avoided emissions from electricity. Meat CBIOs represented 13%, 23% and 20% of total CBIOs in scenarios Sug_feed, Corn_feed and Soy_feed, respectively. The inclusion of corn during sugarcane offseason and the production of bio- diesel in Soy_feed can double CBIO revenues compared to ug_past and Sug_feed revenues.

CBIO revenues from biofuels represented only up to 2% of total rev- enues and it increased up to 3% when considering avoided GHG emis- sions from electricity and meat. However, the current CBIO price in Brazil is only 8.4 USD (B3, 2021) and, in a context of implementation of decarbonization programs to reduce GHG emissions from energy sec- tor (Souza et al., 2021b), carbon credits prices can considerably increase and perhaps be the necessary incentive for a wide implementation of BLI systems in Brazil, Just as a perspective, carbon taxes considered in the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs), for example, can reach up to 260 USD in 2050 in SSP 2.6 (Riahi et al., 2017).

Finally, replacing all diesel demand for agricultural operations and transport of inputs and feedstocks in Soy_feed and considering no costs and emissions are allocated to this product that is internally pro- duced and consumed, there is a positive impact on techno-economic and GHG mitigation potential of this BLI system (Fig. S9, Supplementary material). Although there is a 52% decrease on biodiesel sales and a slight increase in biodiesel carbon intensity (7%), all remaining parame- ters presented positive results. Biodiesel carbon intensity increased since all soybean-related emissions are allocated to less biodiesel being sold. Replacing diesel with biodiesel on agricultural operations re- duced GHG emissions of sugarcane ethanol, electricity, corn ethanol and meat in 12%, 12%, 3% and 1%, respectively. Operational costs and pay- back time decreased 14% and 16%, respectively, purchase of fossil diesel was not required. For the same reason, gross profit, IRR and NPV in- creased 21%, 15% and 35%, respectively.

To overcome possible uncertainties and limitations from the as- sumptions considered in this study, we performed a sensitivity analysis to understand how variation of key parameters could impact on techno-

economic and environmental performance of BLI systems. We selected seven key parameters for the sensitivity analysis based on their contri- bution to the overall results and their intrinsic uncertainty and variabil- ity. They are sugarcane yields, recovery of N content from manure applied in sugarcane field, cattle stocking rate capacity, carcass yield, av- erage daily gains in feedlot systems, total investments, and sugarcane
Stocking rate
N recovery
ADG
Total investment

|30 a)|40|b) |25 20 15 10 5 0|25 30 35 20 15 10 5 0| ||25 30 35 20 15 10 5 0| 250 c)|250 c)|350%|d) |200 150 100 50|300% 250% 200% 150% 100% 50% 0%| ||| |7 e)|500|f) |6 5 4 3 2 1 0|450 400 350 300 250 200 150 100 50|f) ||0| Sugarcane yield Bagasse for feed Carcass yield|Sugarcane yield Bagasse for feed Carcass yield|Sugarcane yield Bagasse for feed Carcass yield|Sugarcane yield Bagasse for feed Carcass yield |||

bagasse for feed composition (Table S17 in the Supplementary mate- rial). Sugarcane yield and cattle stocking rate capacity are site specific, and a large variability is expected. Recovery of N content from manure, average daily gain, and carcass yield can vary depending on feed compo- sition and cattle management. Amount of bagasse for feed production can vary depending on market conditions that can benefit electricity generation, for example. Finally, as the total investments have great in- fluence on techno-economic performance, it was a key parameter to be included in this sensitivity assessment. The lower and upper bounds of these variations were placed from -50% to 50% in relation to the default values used in scenario Soy_feed, the case with the highest integration.

From the seven key parameters, variation of sugarcane yield contrib- uted the most to all outputs considered (Fig. 5). Bagasse content on feed, on the other hand, had almost no impact in five out of the six outputs considered, it only considerably impacted on surplus electricity sold to the grid, since more bagasse for feed means less LCM available for CHP. The same effect holds true for variable stocking rate, since more cattle heads per hectare means more bagasse for feed and less for CHP. Cattle stocking rate also impacted on total land use, as higher stocking rates means lower land demand. As expected, higher carcass yields produced more meat and reduced GHG emissions associated with it, however these parameters did not affect other outputs. Average daily gain (ADG) slightly impacted on GHG emissions of sugarcane eth- anol because lower feedlot duration allowed a higher production of electricity, and more emissions were allocated to it. When more elec- tricity is produced NPV/investments increases as well. As expected, var- iation in total investments had the highest impacts on NPV/investments ratio indicator. Recovery of N on manure application on the field had al- most no impact in all assessed outputs.

The climate change mitigation potential of BLI systems can be im- pacted when accounting for land use change (LUC) emissions of crops expansion on released pastureland. A sensitivity assessment was per- formed considering four set of approaches to account for soil carbon emission changes and to understand their impact on total GHG emis- sions per hectare in all BLI scenarios. The Approach 1 considers the de- fault guidelines of Directive 2009/28/ECLUC (EC, 2010); Approach 2 considers LUC emissions as calculated by Chagas et al. (2016), consider- ing that expansion of crops (i.e., sugarcane and corn/soybean) happens on degraded pastureland; Approach 3 is similar to Approach 2, but ex- pansion happens in severely degraded pastureland; Approach 4 con- siders LUC emissions only from sugarcane expansion on pasture calculated by Picoli (2017). Land use change emissions were calculated according to IPCC (2006) guidelines, applying the equations detailed in Table S18 Section 3.2 of Supplementary Material. As result, accounting for LUC emissions of sugarcane and soybean/corn expansion on pasture- land could increase total GHG emissions per hectare up to 47% in Soy_feed in Approach 1 and reduce them up to 19% in Approach 3 (Fig. depending on soil organic carbon (SOC) and pas- ture condition before biofuel expansion. Corn and soybean are annual crops and are reported to have a lower SOC than pasture in all LUC changes accounting approaches. Therefore, expansion of these crops in pasture area are likely to cause relatively higher LUC compared with sugarcane expansion. However, LUC emissions strongly depend on site-specific edaphoclimatic conditions of the current and previous land uses (Lal, 2010) and require a deeper and more detailed assess- ment to be performed in future work.

Under the considered conditions, BLI scenarios can be economically feasible but high economic uncertainties are associated with the inte- gration (Souza and Seabra, 2014). A minimum acceptable rate of return per year of 12% was considered and any NPV above 0 represents the eco- nomic feasibility, which happened in all scenarios. Furthermore, fast paybacks are desirable in any enterprise. However, this 12% is accept- able for the sugarcane sector in conventional production pathways (Watanabe et al., 2016). In order to explore possibilities to improve eco- nomic feasibility of BLI systems, we performed one sensitivity assess- ments to account for extra revenues from carbon credit generation

and one to account for benefits when replacing diesel with biodiesel. Avoided GHG emissions from BLI systems could generate extra reve- nues in terms of carbon credits. For example, each tonne of avoided GHG emissions is equivalent to one decarbonization credit unit (CBIO) in RenovaBio program (MME, n.d.), and its market price is considered as 8.4 USD (B3, 2021). Although RenovaBio currently considers only CBIOs from liquid biofuels used in transport sector, we calculated possi- ble avoided GHG emissions also from cattle and electricity production as an indication for the monetization of their climate benefits. For cattle, the carbon intensity of meat in pasture is compared to meat in feedlot, calculated in this study; sugarcane electricity is compared with carbon intensity of natural gas electricity of 500 gCO2eq/kWh (Ecoinvent, 2018). Accounting for CBIOs generation from biofuels would increase overall revenues up to 14 M USD in Soy_fee (Fig. S8 in the Supplemen- tary Material). From the 9 million USD/year of Soy_feed considering only CBIO-biofuels, sugarcane electricity and meat could contribute to an additional 1.6 and 2.7 million USD, respectively, totalizing 13.38 mil- lion USD of CBIO revenues. The sugarcane related CBIOs increased 2 to 3 million USD per year when considering additional avoided emissions from electricity. Meat CBIOs represented 13%, 23% and 20% of total CBIOs in scenarios Sug_feed, Corn_feed and Soy_feed, respectively. The inclusion of corn during sugarcane offseason and the production of bio- diesel in Soy_feed can double CBIO revenues compared to ug_past and Sug_feed revenues.

CBIO revenues from biofuels represented only up to 2% of total rev- enues and it increased up to 3% when considering avoided GHG emis- sions from electricity and meat. However, the current CBIO price in Brazil is only 8.4 USD (B3, 2021) and, in a context of implementation of decarbonization programs to reduce GHG emissions from energy sec- tor (Souza et al., 2021b), carbon credits prices can considerably increase and perhaps be the necessary incentive for a wide implementation of BLI systems in Brazil, Just as a perspective, carbon taxes considered in the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs), for example, can reach up to 260 USD in 2050 in SSP 2.6 (Riahi et al., 2017).

Finally, replacing all diesel demand for agricultural operations and transport of inputs and feedstocks in Soy_feed and considering no costs and emissions are allocated to this product that is internally pro- duced and consumed, there is a positive impact on techno-economic and GHG mitigation potential of this BLI system (Fig. S9, Supplementary material). Although there is a 52% decrease on biodiesel sales and a slight increase in biodiesel carbon intensity (7%), all remaining parame- ters presented positive results. Biodiesel carbon intensity increased since all soybean-related emissions are allocated to less biodiesel being sold. Replacing diesel with biodiesel on agricultural operations re- duced GHG emissions of sugarcane ethanol, electricity, corn ethanol and meat in 12%, 12%, 3% and 1%, respectively. Operational costs and pay- back time decreased 14% and 16%, respectively, purchase of fossil diesel was not required. For the same reason, gross profit, IRR and NPV in- creased 21%, 15% and 35%, respectively.
40: <header>3.2. Comparison of BLI and conventional systems towards SDGs </header>

We interpreted the LCA results of conventional and BLI systems under an SDGs context for sugarcane ethanol and meat, two common outputs in all scenarios (Fig. 4). The arithmetic mean of the results from the four conventional and three BLI scenarios are applied. This is justified as the differences among them were only about 1% to 4%. Abso- lute LCA values in each category are presented in Table S16 of the Supplementary Material.

For sugarcane ethanol, conventional scenarios presented higher im- pacts than BLI scenarios in eight out of nine addressed SDGs. In SDGs Zero Hunger and SDG 3 - Good Health and Well-Being, BLI systems pre- sented lower impacts, mostly due to allocation to more products. Higher LCM burnt in boilers in conventional scenarios contributed to 10% higher terrestrial acidification and 9% higher fine particular matter for- mation, which can negatively impact on sustainable agricultural pro- duction (SDG 2) and air pollution, possibly leading to respiratory diseases (SDG 3). Higher mineral N fertilizer (i.e., urea) use led to a 27% higher freshwater eutrophication in conventional scenarios and can negatively impact on freshwater quality (SDG 6 - Clean Water and Sanitation). In SDG 7 - Affordable and Clean Energy, conventional scenarios used 21% more non-renewable resources, resulting in a higher fossil resource scarcity due to higher urea production and transporta- tion. In SDG 11 - Sustainable Cities and Communities, conventional sce- narios performed 17% worst due to higher ozone formation during urea production, leading to potential negative impacts in air quality. Compar- ing mineral resource scarcity, the indicator for SDG 12 - Responsible Consumption and Production, the conventional scenario presented 21% higher impacts due to larger CHP units since no LCM is diverted for feed purposes. Sugarcane ethanol and meat presented about 17% and 23% lower GHG emissions, respectively, when compared to conven- tional systems, having a better performance in SDG 13 - Climate Action. This is mostly due to allocation of overall emissions to feed components. All cattle are finished in pasture management and 20% more land is OC- cupied in conventional systems, leading to higher impacts in SDG 15 Life On Land. However, this 20% accounts for land use to produce the in- puts used in each scenario, as defined by ReCiPe 2016 method. In terms of occupied land, BLI systems could produce the same products as con- ventional systems using about half area (Table S15 in the Supplemen- tary material), what could lead to an even better performance towards SDG 15. SDG 14 - Life Below Water is the only SDG where conventional systems performed better than BLI (8%), mostly because of higher N- related emissions from manure application on sugarcane fields that led to higher marine eutrophication.
Some tradeoffs are observed comparing livestock in conventional and BLI systems. From the nine SDGs assessed in this work, conventional

3.3. Sensitivity analysis: maximization of BLI techno-economic and environmental performance

To overcome possible uncertainties and limitations from the as- sumptions considered in this study, we performed a sensitivity analysis to understand how variation of key parameters could impact on techno-

economic and environmental performance of BLI systems. We selected seven key parameters for the sensitivity analysis based on their contri- bution to the overall results and their intrinsic uncertainty and variabil- ity. They are sugarcane yields, recovery of N content from manure applied in sugarcane field, cattle stocking rate capacity, carcass yield, av- erage daily gains in feedlot systems, total investments, and sugarcane
Stocking rate
N recovery
ADG
Total investment

|30 a)|40|b) |25 20 15 10 5 0|25 30 35 20 15 10 5 0| ||25 30 35 20 15 10 5 0| 250 c)|250 c)|350%|d) |200 150 100 50|300% 250% 200% 150% 100% 50% 0%| ||| |7 e)|500|f) |6 5 4 3 2 1 0|450 400 350 300 250 200 150 100 50|f) ||0| Sugarcane yield Bagasse for feed Carcass yield|Sugarcane yield Bagasse for feed Carcass yield|Sugarcane yield Bagasse for feed Carcass yield|Sugarcane yield Bagasse for feed Carcass yield |||

bagasse for feed composition (Table S17 in the Supplementary mate- rial). Sugarcane yield and cattle stocking rate capacity are site specific, and a large variability is expected. Recovery of N content from manure, average daily gain, and carcass yield can vary depending on feed compo- sition and cattle management. Amount of bagasse for feed production can vary depending on market conditions that can benefit electricity generation, for example. Finally, as the total investments have great in- fluence on techno-economic performance, it was a key parameter to be included in this sensitivity assessment. The lower and upper bounds of these variations were placed from -50% to 50% in relation to the default values used in scenario Soy_feed, the case with the highest integration.

From the seven key parameters, variation of sugarcane yield contrib- uted the most to all outputs considered (Fig. 5). Bagasse content on feed, on the other hand, had almost no impact in five out of the six outputs considered, it only considerably impacted on surplus electricity sold to the grid, since more bagasse for feed means less LCM available for CHP. The same effect holds true for variable stocking rate, since more cattle heads per hectare means more bagasse for feed and less for CHP. Cattle stocking rate also impacted on total land use, as higher stocking rates means lower land demand. As expected, higher carcass yields produced more meat and reduced GHG emissions associated with it, however these parameters did not affect other outputs. Average daily gain (ADG) slightly impacted on GHG emissions of sugarcane eth- anol because lower feedlot duration allowed a higher production of electricity, and more emissions were allocated to it. When more elec- tricity is produced NPV/investments increases as well. As expected, var- iation in total investments had the highest impacts on NPV/investments ratio indicator. Recovery of N on manure application on the field had al- most no impact in all assessed outputs.

The climate change mitigation potential of BLI systems can be im- pacted when accounting for land use change (LUC) emissions of crops expansion on released pastureland. A sensitivity assessment was per- formed considering four set of approaches to account for soil carbon emission changes and to understand their impact on total GHG emis- sions per hectare in all BLI scenarios. The Approach 1 considers the de- fault guidelines of Directive 2009/28/ECLUC (EC, 2010); Approach 2 considers LUC emissions as calculated by Chagas et al. (2016), consider- ing that expansion of crops (i.e., sugarcane and corn/soybean) happens on degraded pastureland; Approach 3 is similar to Approach 2, but ex- pansion happens in severely degraded pastureland; Approach 4 con- siders LUC emissions only from sugarcane expansion on pasture calculated by Picoli (2017). Land use change emissions were calculated according to IPCC (2006) guidelines, applying the equations detailed in Table S18 Section 3.2 of Supplementary Material. As result, accounting for LUC emissions of sugarcane and soybean/corn expansion on pasture- land could increase total GHG emissions per hectare up to 47% in Soy_feed in Approach 1 and reduce them up to 19% in Approach 3 (Fig. depending on soil organic carbon (SOC) and pas- ture condition before biofuel expansion. Corn and soybean are annual crops and are reported to have a lower SOC than pasture in all LUC changes accounting approaches. Therefore, expansion of these crops in pasture area are likely to cause relatively higher LUC compared with sugarcane expansion. However, LUC emissions strongly depend on site-specific edaphoclimatic conditions of the current and previous land uses (Lal, 2010) and require a deeper and more detailed assess- ment to be performed in future work.

Under the considered conditions, BLI scenarios can be economically feasible but high economic uncertainties are associated with the inte- gration (Souza and Seabra, 2014). A minimum acceptable rate of return per year of 12% was considered and any NPV above 0 represents the eco- nomic feasibility, which happened in all scenarios. Furthermore, fast paybacks are desirable in any enterprise. However, this 12% is accept- able for the sugarcane sector in conventional production pathways (Watanabe et al., 2016). In order to explore possibilities to improve eco- nomic feasibility of BLI systems, we performed one sensitivity assess- ments to account for extra revenues from carbon credit generation

and one to account for benefits when replacing diesel with biodiesel. Avoided GHG emissions from BLI systems could generate extra reve- nues in terms of carbon credits. For example, each tonne of avoided GHG emissions is equivalent to one decarbonization credit unit (CBIO) in RenovaBio program (MME, n.d.), and its market price is considered as 8.4 USD (B3, 2021). Although RenovaBio currently considers only CBIOs from liquid biofuels used in transport sector, we calculated possi- ble avoided GHG emissions also from cattle and electricity production as an indication for the monetization of their climate benefits. For cattle, the carbon intensity of meat in pasture is compared to meat in feedlot, calculated in this study; sugarcane electricity is compared with carbon intensity of natural gas electricity of 500 gCO2eq/kWh (Ecoinvent, 2018). Accounting for CBIOs generation from biofuels would increase overall revenues up to 14 M USD in Soy_fee (Fig. S8 in the Supplemen- tary Material). From the 9 million USD/year of Soy_feed considering only CBIO-biofuels, sugarcane electricity and meat could contribute to an additional 1.6 and 2.7 million USD, respectively, totalizing 13.38 mil- lion USD of CBIO revenues. The sugarcane related CBIOs increased 2 to 3 million USD per year when considering additional avoided emissions from electricity. Meat CBIOs represented 13%, 23% and 20% of total CBIOs in scenarios Sug_feed, Corn_feed and Soy_feed, respectively. The inclusion of corn during sugarcane offseason and the production of bio- diesel in Soy_feed can double CBIO revenues compared to ug_past and Sug_feed revenues.

CBIO revenues from biofuels represented only up to 2% of total rev- enues and it increased up to 3% when considering avoided GHG emis- sions from electricity and meat. However, the current CBIO price in Brazil is only 8.4 USD (B3, 2021) and, in a context of implementation of decarbonization programs to reduce GHG emissions from energy sec- tor (Souza et al., 2021b), carbon credits prices can considerably increase and perhaps be the necessary incentive for a wide implementation of BLI systems in Brazil, Just as a perspective, carbon taxes considered in the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs), for example, can reach up to 260 USD in 2050 in SSP 2.6 (Riahi et al., 2017).

Finally, replacing all diesel demand for agricultural operations and transport of inputs and feedstocks in Soy_feed and considering no costs and emissions are allocated to this product that is internally pro- duced and consumed, there is a positive impact on techno-economic and GHG mitigation potential of this BLI system (Fig. S9, Supplementary material). Although there is a 52% decrease on biodiesel sales and a slight increase in biodiesel carbon intensity (7%), all remaining parame- ters presented positive results. Biodiesel carbon intensity increased since all soybean-related emissions are allocated to less biodiesel being sold. Replacing diesel with biodiesel on agricultural operations re- duced GHG emissions of sugarcane ethanol, electricity, corn ethanol and meat in 12%, 12%, 3% and 1%, respectively. Operational costs and pay- back time decreased 14% and 16%, respectively, purchase of fossil diesel was not required. For the same reason, gross profit, IRR and NPV in- creased 21%, 15% and 35%, respectively.
42: <header>3.2. Comparison of BLI and conventional systems towards SDGs </header>

We interpreted the LCA results of conventional and BLI systems under an SDGs context for sugarcane ethanol and meat, two common outputs in all scenarios (Fig. 4). The arithmetic mean of the results from the four conventional and three BLI scenarios are applied. This is justified as the differences among them were only about 1% to 4%. Abso- lute LCA values in each category are presented in Table S16 of the Supplementary Material.

For sugarcane ethanol, conventional scenarios presented higher im- pacts than BLI scenarios in eight out of nine addressed SDGs. In SDGs Zero Hunger and SDG 3 - Good Health and Well-Being, BLI systems pre- sented lower impacts, mostly due to allocation to more products. Higher LCM burnt in boilers in conventional scenarios contributed to 10% higher terrestrial acidification and 9% higher fine particular matter for- mation, which can negatively impact on sustainable agricultural pro- duction (SDG 2) and air pollution, possibly leading to respiratory diseases (SDG 3). Higher mineral N fertilizer (i.e., urea) use led to a 27% higher freshwater eutrophication in conventional scenarios and can negatively impact on freshwater quality (SDG 6 - Clean Water and Sanitation). In SDG 7 - Affordable and Clean Energy, conventional scenarios used 21% more non-renewable resources, resulting in a higher fossil resource scarcity due to higher urea production and transporta- tion. In SDG 11 - Sustainable Cities and Communities, conventional sce- narios performed 17% worst due to higher ozone formation during urea production, leading to potential negative impacts in air quality. Compar- ing mineral resource scarcity, the indicator for SDG 12 - Responsible Consumption and Production, the conventional scenario presented 21% higher impacts due to larger CHP units since no LCM is diverted for feed purposes. Sugarcane ethanol and meat presented about 17% and 23% lower GHG emissions, respectively, when compared to conven- tional systems, having a better performance in SDG 13 - Climate Action. This is mostly due to allocation of overall emissions to feed components. All cattle are finished in pasture management and 20% more land is OC- cupied in conventional systems, leading to higher impacts in SDG 15 Life On Land. However, this 20% accounts for land use to produce the in- puts used in each scenario, as defined by ReCiPe 2016 method. In terms of occupied land, BLI systems could produce the same products as con- ventional systems using about half area (Table S15 in the Supplemen- tary material), what could lead to an even better performance towards SDG 15. SDG 14 - Life Below Water is the only SDG where conventional systems performed better than BLI (8%), mostly because of higher N- related emissions from manure application on sugarcane fields that led to higher marine eutrophication.
Some tradeoffs are observed comparing livestock in conventional and BLI systems. From the nine SDGs assessed in this work, conventional
