bagasse for feed composition (Table S17 in the Supplementary mate- rial). Sugarcane yield and cattle stocking rate capacity are site specific, and a large variability is expected. Recovery of N content from manure, average daily gain, and carcass yield can vary depending on feed compo- sition and cattle management. Amount of bagasse for feed production can vary depending on market conditions that can benefit electricity generation, for example. Finally, as the total investments have great in- fluence on techno-economic performance, it was a key parameter to be included in this sensitivity assessment. The lower and upper bounds of these variations were placed from -50% to 50% in relation to the default values used in scenario Soy_feed, the case with the highest integration.

From the seven key parameters, variation of sugarcane yield contrib- uted the most to all outputs considered (Fig. 5). Bagasse content on feed, on the other hand, had almost no impact in five out of the six outputs considered, it only considerably impacted on surplus electricity sold to the grid, since more bagasse for feed means less LCM available for CHP. The same effect holds true for variable stocking rate, since more cattle heads per hectare means more bagasse for feed and less for CHP. Cattle stocking rate also impacted on total land use, as higher stocking rates means lower land demand. As expected, higher carcass yields produced more meat and reduced GHG emissions associated with it, however these parameters did not affect other outputs. Average daily gain (ADG) slightly impacted on GHG emissions of sugarcane eth- anol because lower feedlot duration allowed a higher production of electricity, and more emissions were allocated to it. When more elec- tricity is produced NPV/investments increases as well. As expected, var- iation in total investments had the highest impacts on NPV/investments ratio indicator. Recovery of N on manure application on the field had al- most no impact in all assessed outputs.

The climate change mitigation potential of BLI systems can be im- pacted when accounting for land use change (LUC) emissions of crops expansion on released pastureland. A sensitivity assessment was per- formed considering four set of approaches to account for soil carbon emission changes and to understand their impact on total GHG emis- sions per hectare in all BLI scenarios. The Approach 1 considers the de- fault guidelines of Directive 2009/28/ECLUC (EC, 2010); Approach 2 considers LUC emissions as calculated by Chagas et al. (2016), consider- ing that expansion of crops (i.e., sugarcane and corn/soybean) happens on degraded pastureland; Approach 3 is similar to Approach 2, but ex- pansion happens in severely degraded pastureland; Approach 4 con- siders LUC emissions only from sugarcane expansion on pasture calculated by Picoli (2017). Land use change emissions were calculated according to IPCC (2006) guidelines, applying the equations detailed in Table S18 Section 3.2 of Supplementary Material. As result, accounting for LUC emissions of sugarcane and soybean/corn expansion on pasture- land could increase total GHG emissions per hectare up to 47% in Soy_feed in Approach 1 and reduce them up to 19% in Approach 3 (Fig. depending on soil organic carbon (SOC) and pas- ture condition before biofuel expansion. Corn and soybean are annual crops and are reported to have a lower SOC than pasture in all LUC changes accounting approaches. Therefore, expansion of these crops in pasture area are likely to cause relatively higher LUC compared with sugarcane expansion. However, LUC emissions strongly depend on site-specific edaphoclimatic conditions of the current and previous land uses (Lal, 2010) and require a deeper and more detailed assess- ment to be performed in future work.

Under the considered conditions, BLI scenarios can be economically feasible but high economic uncertainties are associated with the inte- gration (Souza and Seabra, 2014). A minimum acceptable rate of return per year of 12% was considered and any NPV above 0 represents the eco- nomic feasibility, which happened in all scenarios. Furthermore, fast paybacks are desirable in any enterprise. However, this 12% is accept- able for the sugarcane sector in conventional production pathways (Watanabe et al., 2016). In order to explore possibilities to improve eco- nomic feasibility of BLI systems, we performed one sensitivity assess- ments to account for extra revenues from carbon credit generation

and one to account for benefits when replacing diesel with biodiesel. Avoided GHG emissions from BLI systems could generate extra reve- nues in terms of carbon credits. For example, each tonne of avoided GHG emissions is equivalent to one decarbonization credit unit (CBIO) in RenovaBio program (MME, n.d.), and its market price is considered as 8.4 USD (B3, 2021). Although RenovaBio currently considers only CBIOs from liquid biofuels used in transport sector, we calculated possi- ble avoided GHG emissions also from cattle and electricity production as an indication for the monetization of their climate benefits. For cattle, the carbon intensity of meat in pasture is compared to meat in feedlot, calculated in this study; sugarcane electricity is compared with carbon intensity of natural gas electricity of 500 gCO2eq/kWh (Ecoinvent, 2018). Accounting for CBIOs generation from biofuels would increase overall revenues up to 14 M USD in Soy_fee (Fig. S8 in the Supplemen- tary Material). From the 9 million USD/year of Soy_feed considering only CBIO-biofuels, sugarcane electricity and meat could contribute to an additional 1.6 and 2.7 million USD, respectively, totalizing 13.38 mil- lion USD of CBIO revenues. The sugarcane related CBIOs increased 2 to 3 million USD per year when considering additional avoided emissions from electricity. Meat CBIOs represented 13%, 23% and 20% of total CBIOs in scenarios Sug_feed, Corn_feed and Soy_feed, respectively. The inclusion of corn during sugarcane offseason and the production of bio- diesel in Soy_feed can double CBIO revenues compared to ug_past and Sug_feed revenues.

CBIO revenues from biofuels represented only up to 2% of total rev- enues and it increased up to 3% when considering avoided GHG emis- sions from electricity and meat. However, the current CBIO price in Brazil is only 8.4 USD (B3, 2021) and, in a context of implementation of decarbonization programs to reduce GHG emissions from energy sec- tor (Souza et al., 2021b), carbon credits prices can considerably increase and perhaps be the necessary incentive for a wide implementation of BLI systems in Brazil, Just as a perspective, carbon taxes considered in the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs), for example, can reach up to 260 USD in 2050 in SSP 2.6 (Riahi et al., 2017).

Finally, replacing all diesel demand for agricultural operations and transport of inputs and feedstocks in Soy_feed and considering no costs and emissions are allocated to this product that is internally pro- duced and consumed, there is a positive impact on techno-economic and GHG mitigation potential of this BLI system (Fig. S9, Supplementary material). Although there is a 52% decrease on biodiesel sales and a slight increase in biodiesel carbon intensity (7%), all remaining parame- ters presented positive results. Biodiesel carbon intensity increased since all soybean-related emissions are allocated to less biodiesel being sold. Replacing diesel with biodiesel on agricultural operations re- duced GHG emissions of sugarcane ethanol, electricity, corn ethanol and meat in 12%, 12%, 3% and 1%, respectively. Operational costs and pay- back time decreased 14% and 16%, respectively, purchase of fossil diesel was not required. For the same reason, gross profit, IRR and NPV in- creased 21%, 15% and 35%, respectively.
55: <header>3.4. Implications of bioenergy-livestock integrated systems in Brazil </header>

The large-scale deployment of bioenergy to meet future energy de- mands and projected climate change mitigation targets must take place in a sustainable way. This includes strategic land use management that does not negatively impact on food production, GHG emissions, human health, biodiversity loss and deforestation, among other poten- tial issues. Worldwide, accounting for biofuels by-products as animal feed can improve land use efficiency and reduce greenhouse gas emis- sions associated with biofuels production (Anderson et al., 2018; Souza et al., 2019). By-products can reduce or replace grains in the animal feed composition, which can improve land use by decreasing dedicated area for grains production (Corré et al., 2016; Mumm et al., 2014). In the US, corn ethanol is a consolidated production chain and by-products from both dry-grind and wet-grind processes can be used as livestock feed, including poultry, swine, beef, and milk cattle (Popp

et al., 2017; Mumm et al., 2014). In Europe, ethanol from wheat or corn can produce different by-products with high nutritional value as animal feed (Popp et al., 2017). Sugar beet ethanol generates beet pulp and stillage that have high nutritional value as animal feed (Buchspies and Kaltschmitt, 2016).

In Brazil, sugarcane molasses, bagasse and yeasts can be used as cat- tle feed for beef and milk production (Egeskog et al., 2011; Souza et al., 2019). Bagasse is inefficiently used in the sector, as it is combusted in obsolete and inefficient boilers, commonly without exports of electricity (Silva al., 2017). It means that just by retrofitting the CHP and recov- ering sugarcane straw from the fields would allow a surplus of bagasse that could be made available for other uses, or by importing alternative energy sources, such as eucalyptus chips. In addition, in the integrated systems, only 7 to 20% of the total bagasse is used as animal feed.

Sugarcane and soybean are consolidated as main feedstock for etha- nol and biodiesel production, and corn ethanol is facing a rapid expan- sion, mostly in the states of Brazilian Midwest region. The by-products from biofuels produced with these three crops can be used to produce animal feed (Egeskog et al., 2011; Popp et al., 2017). The present study aims to enhance the understanding and foster the implementa- tion of BLI systems in Brazil by assessing their potential to improved techno-economic and environmental feasibility when compared to con- ventional systems, highlighting improvements in land use and conse- quent increase in avoided GHG emissions per unit of production area. These systems can co-produce energy and food, alleviating pressure on land resources in both bioenergy and livestock sectors.

Although economic feasibility is key for wide application of BLI in Brazil, until these systems are fully developed and robust, the environ- mental advantages (e.g., GHG mitigation) should boost BLI systems in the country. The results presented here show BLI systems could contrib- ute to future energy demands and to climate change mitigation targets committed in the Paris Agreement and to SDGs. It is worthwhile men- tioning that Brazil just committed to reduce methane emissions during United Nations Climate Change Conference (COP 26) (CNN, 2021), and BLI systems decreased overall methane emissions in 81% when com- pared to conventional systems.

Among the challenges and limitations to a broader application of BLI systems in Brazil are possible competition of sugarcane bagasse for feed, electricity and cellulosic ethanol purposes, the need for of financial in- centives, operational complexity and required specific know-how, due to the additional value chains involved and the diversified portfolio of products (Souza et al., 2021a). BLI can be further developed to improve their techno-economic and environmental benefits in Brazil, such as in- cluding codigestion of sugarcane vinasse and cattle manure, considering alternative industrial crops such as macauba, sweet sorghum, energy cane and short rotation eucalyptus coppice as feed and/or energy sources (Souza et al., 2021a), and integrating with other biofuel path- ways, such as thermochemical and second-generation pathways.

Future studies could explore different LCA approaches, such as con- sidering credit generation of co-products used as animal feed (Anderson et al., 2018; Popp et al., 2017). Other economic perspectives should be explored, including incremental (brownfield) assessment of integrated systems in existing biofuel refineries, horizontal assessment considering livestock and biofuel as two different enterprises (Souza et al., 2019), considering return rates that more closely represents integrated sys- tems and including potential positive externalities of having a more di- verse landscape. Social perspective could be assessed via Social Life Cycle Assessment (S-LCA), input-output analysis (IOA), and hybrid S- LCA and IOA. Also, explore other livestock systems such as full-cycle and/or recovery of degraded pasture, as it is likely that not all cattle can be fed in feedlots and cow-calf system has limitations to be raised in feedlot systems (Souza et al., 2019; USGC, 2012). Recovery of de- graded pasture by intensifying livestock production is a promising op- tion for releasing pasture area for bioenergy crops (Silva et al., 2017), it can also increase carbon sequestration, decrease overall GHG emis- sions per unit of meat produced, and avoid deforestation (Cardoso

et al., 2016; Silva et al., 2017; Figueiredo et al., 2017). Finally, benefit from spatially explicit assessments to explore BLI sustainability and im- plications, since bioenergy sustainability impacts depend on regional and local characteristics (Hiloidhari et al., 2017); such as impacts on water use and water availability, on biodiversity and on biomes.

This includes strategic land use management that does not negatively impact on food production, GHG emissions, human health, biodiversity loss and deforestation, among other poten- tial issues. By-products can reduce or replace grains in the animal feed composition, which can improve land use by decreasing dedicated area for grains production (Corré et al., 2016; Mumm et al., 2014). In the US, corn ethanol is a consolidated production chain and by-products from both dry-grind and wet-grind processes can be used as livestock feed, including poultry, swine, beef, and milk cattle (Popp

et al., 2017; Mumm et al., 2014). In Europe, ethanol from wheat or corn can produce different by-products with high nutritional value as animal feed (Popp et al., 2017). Sugar beet ethanol generates beet pulp and stillage that have high nutritional value as animal feed (Buchspies and Kaltschmitt, 2016).

In Brazil, sugarcane molasses, bagasse and yeasts can be used as cat- tle feed for beef and milk production (Egeskog et al., 2011; Souza et al., 2019). Bagasse is inefficiently used in the sector, as it is combusted in obsolete and inefficient boilers, commonly without exports of electricity (Silva al., 2017). It means that just by retrofitting the CHP and recov- ering sugarcane straw from the fields would allow a surplus of bagasse that could be made available for other uses, or by importing alternative energy sources, such as eucalyptus chips. In addition, in the integrated systems, only 7 to 20% of the total bagasse is used as animal feed.

Sugarcane and soybean are consolidated as main feedstock for etha- nol and biodiesel production, and corn ethanol is facing a rapid expan- sion, mostly in the states of Brazilian Midwest region. The by-products from biofuels produced with these three crops can be used to produce animal feed (Egeskog et al., 2011; Popp et al., 2017). The present study aims to enhance the understanding and foster the implementa- tion of BLI systems in Brazil by assessing their potential to improved techno-economic and environmental feasibility when compared to con- ventional systems, highlighting improvements in land use and conse- quent increase in avoided GHG emissions per unit of production area. These systems can co-produce energy and food, alleviating pressure on land resources in both bioenergy and livestock sectors.

Although economic feasibility is key for wide application of BLI in Brazil, until these systems are fully developed and robust, the environ- mental advantages (e.g., GHG mitigation) should boost BLI systems in the country. The results presented here show BLI systems could contrib- ute to future energy demands and to climate change mitigation targets committed in the Paris Agreement and to SDGs. It is worthwhile men- tioning that Brazil just committed to reduce methane emissions during United Nations Climate Change Conference (COP 26) (CNN, 2021), and BLI systems decreased overall methane emissions in 81% when com- pared to conventional systems.

Among the challenges and limitations to a broader application of BLI systems in Brazil are possible competition of sugarcane bagasse for feed, electricity and cellulosic ethanol purposes, the need for of financial in- centives, operational complexity and required specific know-how, due to the additional value chains involved and the diversified portfolio of products (Souza et al., 2021a). BLI can be further developed to improve their techno-economic and environmental benefits in Brazil, such as in- cluding codigestion of sugarcane vinasse and cattle manure, considering alternative industrial crops such as macauba, sweet sorghum, energy cane and short rotation eucalyptus coppice as feed and/or energy sources (Souza et al., 2021a), and integrating with other biofuel path- ways, such as thermochemical and second-generation pathways.

Future studies could explore different LCA approaches, such as con- sidering credit generation of co-products used as animal feed (Anderson et al., 2018; Popp et al., 2017). Other economic perspectives should be explored, including incremental (brownfield) assessment of integrated systems in existing biofuel refineries, horizontal assessment considering livestock and biofuel as two different enterprises (Souza et al., 2019), considering return rates that more closely represents integrated sys- tems and including potential positive externalities of having a more di- verse landscape. Social perspective could be assessed via Social Life Cycle Assessment (S-LCA), input-output analysis (IOA), and hybrid S- LCA and IOA. Also, explore other livestock systems such as full-cycle and/or recovery of degraded pasture, as it is likely that not all cattle can be fed in feedlots and cow-calf system has limitations to be raised in feedlot systems (Souza et al., 2019; USGC, 2012). Recovery of de- graded pasture by intensifying livestock production is a promising op- tion for releasing pasture area for bioenergy crops (Silva et al., 2017), it can also increase carbon sequestration, decrease overall GHG emis- sions per unit of meat produced, and avoid deforestation (Cardoso

et al., 2016; Silva et al., 2017; Figueiredo et al., 2017). Finally, benefit from spatially explicit assessments to explore BLI sustainability and im- plications, since bioenergy sustainability impacts depend on regional and local characteristics (Hiloidhari et al., 2017); such as impacts on water use and water availability, on biodiversity and on biomes.

62: This study provided a better understanding of the technical, eco- nomic and environmental impacts of BLI scenarios, highlighting the synergies related to land use and economic effects of using bioenergy by-products as animal feed. Integrated systems are an important option for future land use management strategies, as they can optimize land use requirements, and reduce climate change and other environmental impacts in comparison to single value chains. Nevertheless, integrating only sugarcane with livestock (Sug_feed) presented best potential to avoid GHG emission per hectare, 2 times higher than conventional sys- tem (Base scenario). On the other hand, the inclusion of corn and soy- bean biofuels (Corn_feed, Soy_feed) resulted in better techno- economic feasibility with a payback time 43% lower, NPV/investment 5 times higher, higher revenues, and IRR 10 percentage points higher than Base scenario.

Going beyond GHG emissions, using bagasse for feed and applica- tion of cattle manure on sugarcane fields replacing mineral urea con- tributed to lower impacts of sugarcane ethanol towards 8 out of the 9 SDGs considered in this study. However, our study unraveled possi- ble tradeoffs when changing cattle from pasture to feedlot towards SDGs. Agricultural production of corn and soybean, that compose feed ingredients, would increase acidification and eutrophication of soil and waters negatively impacting on SDG 2, 6, and 14, and in- creasing mineral resource scarcity (SDG 12). On the other hand, meat produced in feedlots would reduce associated impacts on SDG 11 and 13, mostly due to shorter cycle duration (120 days) and no in- puts from pasture management. In the other SDGs, both systems pre- sented similar results.

These results are expected to contribute to the challenges of land- based climate change mitigation strategies and growing demand of food, feed, and energy in Brazil and globally. This study might help to in- centivize a broad integration of biofuels and livestock sectors in Brazil as BLI systems can contribute to achieve GHG mitigation targets stipulated by the country in the Paris Agreement and to the SDGs, without compromising meat production and with techno-economic feasibility. There is still room to explore BLI implementation in Brazil such as max- imize synergies, as vinasse and manure co-digestion, inclusion of other biofuel routes (e.g., thermochemical, 2G), mitigate or reduce tradeoffs by improving feedlot performance towards SDGs, assess impacts on water use, consider complete cycle of livestock production, inclusion of alternative crops as animal feed or energy source (e.g., macauba, sor- ghum, energy cane, eucalyptus). We suggest further studies to explore alternative environmental and economic assessments and to account for site-specific crop yields, effects on soil organic carbon and land use change emissions in a spatially explicit sustainability assessment of BLI expansion in Brazil.
